blocks.html:
PRESENTATION Vbis
COHOMOLOGICAL DESCENT TECHNIQUESby B. Saint-Donat(∗)

footnotes.html: (∗) According to brief notes by P. Deligne.

blocks.html: Introduction

1. Let \(X\) be a topological space and \(\mathfrak{u} = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) a cover of \(X\), that one assumes either open or closed and locally finite. If \(\mathfrak{F}\) is a beam abelian on \(X\), the Leray spectral sequence: \[ (1.1) \qquad \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{u}, \mathscr{H}^q(\mathfrak{F})) \Longrightarrow H^*(X, \mathfrak{F}) \] defined by \(\mathfrak{u}\) [[5] II (5.2.4) and (5.4.1)] can be described as follows:

The overlap \(\mathfrak{u}\) defines a "Čechiste" resolution \(C^*(\mathfrak{u}, \mathfrak{F})\), functorial in \(\mathfrak{F}\) (ibid 5.2.1). On the other hand, we have, for all \(\mathfrak{F}\) a resolution "canonical flange", \(C^*(\mathfrak{F})\), functorial in \(\mathfrak{F}\) (ibid (4.3)). With these notations, the spectral sequence (1.1) is obtained, in the case where \(\mathfrak{u}\) is open, from the double complex \[ (1.2) \qquad \Gamma(X, C^*(\mathfrak{u}, C^*(\mathfrak{F}))) . \] In the case where \(\mathfrak{u}\) is closed and locally finite, we consider the double complex \[ (1.3) \qquad \Gamma(X, C^*(C^*(\mathfrak{u}, \mathfrak{F}))) . \]

2. Let us look for a unified description of these double complexes. Let us denote by \(X_o\) the topological space disjoint sum of \(U_i\) and by \(X_n\) (\(n \geq 0\)) the fiber product iterated \((n+1)^{\text{ième}}\) from \(X_o\) with itself on top of \(X\): \[ (2.1) \qquad X_n = \coprod_{i_0 \dots i_n \in I} U_{i_0} \cap \dots \cap U_{i_n} = \coprod_{\sigma \in \mathrm{Hom}([n], I)} \bigcap_{i=0}^{n} U_{\sigma(i)} . \] The \(X_n\) form a simplicial system of topological spaces, and if \(j_n\) denotes the projection of \(X_n\) onto \(X\), we have \[ (2.2) \qquad C^n(\mathfrak{u}, \mathfrak{F}) = j_{n*}\, j_n^*(\mathfrak{F}) . \] Note that the formation of canonical flaccid resolutions commutes to the restriction to an open and direct image by a closed immersion. From then on:

1. a)

    if \(\mathfrak{u}\) is open \[ (2.3) \qquad C^q(\mathfrak{u}, C^p(\mathfrak{F})) = j_{q*}\, j_q^*\, C^p(\mathfrak{F}) = j_{q*}\, C^p(j_q^* \mathfrak{F}) \]

  2. b)

    if \(\mathfrak{u}\) is closed and locally finished \[ (2.4) \qquad C^p(C^q(\mathfrak{u}, \mathfrak{F})) = C^p(j_{q*}\, j_q^* \mathfrak{F}) = j_{q*}\, C^p(j_q^* \mathfrak{F}) . \]

Thus, to obtain a unified description of (1.2) and (1.3), we see that it suffices to take the "canonical flaccid" resolution of \(j_q^*(\mathfrak{F})\) to \(X_q\) for all \(q\), then apply the functor \(j_{q*}\) to this resolution.

3. The preceding description retains meaning for any simplicial system of spaces topologies above \(X\): \[ (3.1) \qquad \Delta^{o} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Top}/X , \qquad [n] \longmapsto X_n \] not necessarily of the form (2.1). However, the double complex, increased by \(\mathfrak{F}\) \[ (3.2) \qquad \mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow \big(j_{q*}\, C^p(j_q^*(\mathfrak{F}))\big)_{p,q} \] will generally not set a resolution of \(\mathfrak{F}\).

This work is devoted to the search for sufficient conditions for that (3.2) defines a resolution of \(\mathfrak{F}\). In this case, the spectral sequence (1.1) generalizes into a spectral sequence \[ (3.3) \qquad \check{H}^p(H^q(X_p, j_p^*(\mathfrak{F}))) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathfrak{F}) \] called “spectral descent sequence”.

In the case of "constant coefficients", analogous spectral sequences have been obtained by Segal (see [8]), by other methods and for other "theories cohomologies", such as K-theory. Segal places himself in the category of C.W. complexes: it uses a “geometric realization” functor which, at a complex semi-simplicial of topological spaces, associates a new topological space; this new space must be compared to the topos associated with a simplicial topos [cf. (1.2.12)].

4. In paragraph 5, we illustrate the criteria obtained by constructing for any analytical space \(X\) on \(\mathbf{C}\), via the resolution of singularities, a simplicial system of non-singular analytic spaces above \(X\), \[ [n] \longmapsto X_n \] such that (3.2) defines a resolution of \(\mathfrak{F}\). If we take for \(\mathfrak{F}\) the beam constant \(\mathbf{C}\), we obtain in particular a spectral sequence \[ (4.1) \qquad H^q(X_p, \mathbf{C}) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathbf{C}) \] which expresses the complex cohomology of \(X\) in terms of the complex cohomology non-singular analytical spaces. Furthermore, if \(X\) is projective, we can assume that all \(X_p\) are projective: this is the essential ingredient which allows to obtain a kind of “Hodge theory” for \(X\) (cf. [2]).
5. The preceding constructions extend as they are when we replace the sheaf \(\mathfrak{F}\) by a lower bounded complex of sheaves. They drive to "localization" techniques in derived categories:

We know that for \(X_o\) given by (2.1), the arrow \[ j_o^* : \mathrm{D}^b(X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^b(X_o) \] is not faithful in general; we will show that a data more precise than that of \(j_o^*(K')\) (for \(K' \in \mathrm{D}^+(X)\)), involving the \(X_n\), sometimes allows you to reconstitute the complex \(K'\).

The statements obtained will be used in the appendix of presentation XVII to extend the definition of the functor \(\mathrm{R} f_!\) (\(f\) separate morphism of finite type between schemas) in case \(f\) is not assumed to be compactifiable.

In this application, it is not possible to only consider topological spaces replaced here by flat sites of diagrams. On the other hand, to carry out the demonstrations successfully, it will be necessary to consider both simplicial systems of spaces as well as multi-simplicial systems. This explains, justifies or excuses the degree of hypergenerality from which we will start.
1. Foreplay
1.1 Notations
(1.1.1) In all that follows, \(\mathcal{U}\) is universe such that \(\mathbf{Z} \in \mathcal{U}\): all topos considered will be \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos.

Let \(T\) and \(T'\) be two topos: a morphism \(\varphi : T \to T'\) consists of the data of a pair of functors \(\varphi_* : T \to T'\) and \(\varphi^* : T' \to T\), provided with a addition \(\mathrm{Hom}_{T'}(., \varphi_*.) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_T(\varphi^*., .)\), such that \(\varphi^*\) is exact on the left (i.e. preserves finite projective limits).

Let \((T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and \((T', \mathcal{O}_{T'})\) be two ringed topos: a morphism of \((T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) in \((T', \mathcal{O}_{T'})\) is a pair \((\varphi, \theta)\) where \(\varphi : T \to T'\) is a topos morphism and \(\theta : \mathcal{O}_{T'} \to \varphi_*(\mathcal{O}_T)\) is a ring morphism.

(1.1.2) We will make constant use of the language of fiber categories as it is set out in [S.G.A. 1VI]; the reader can also refer to [4]. Let's fix simply a few notations: if \(E \to B\) is a fiber functor (resp. cofibrant), for a morphism \(m : i \to j\) in \(B\), we will note \(m^* : E_j \to E_i\) (resp. \(m_* : E_i \to E_j\)) the "reciprocal image" functor (resp. “direct image”) associated with it; each of these functors is defined up to a single functional isomorphism. If \(\varphi : E \to E'\) is a \(B\)-functor, for any object \(i\) of \(B\), we will note \(\varphi_i : E_i \to E'_i\) the restriction functor from \(\varphi\) to \(E_i\).

(1.1.3) Finally, we will designate by \(\Delta\) the following category: the objects of \(\Delta\) are the ordered sets \([n] = \{0, 1, \dots, n\}\) and the morphisms of \(\Delta\) are all growing applications (in the broad sense). \(\Delta^+\) (resp. \(\Delta^-\)) will designate the category whose objects are those of \(\Delta\) and whose arrows are the monomorphisms (resp. epimorphisms) of \(\Delta\). We will use freely and as and when required, the classical notations introduced about \(\Delta\) [cf. [3] II 2].
1.2 D-topos

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (1.2.1)

Let \(D\) be a category and \(E \to D\) be a fibrating functor and cofibrant. We will say that \(E\) is bifibred in topos above \(D\) wherever \(E\) is a \(D\)-topos if the following conditions are met:

  1. (a)

    For any object \(i\) of \(D\) the fiber category \(E_i\) is a topos.

  2. (b)

    For every morphism \(m : i \to j\) in \(D\), there exists a topos morphism \(f : E_j \to E_i\) such as \(f_* = m^*\) and \(f^* = m_*\).
Remark. Condition (b) can still be expressed, taking into account (a), by saying that the functor \(m_*\) is left exact.
When \(D = \Delta\) (resp. \(\Delta \times \Delta\)) we will speak of simplicial topos (resp. simplicial double) to designate a \(\Delta\)-topos (resp. a \(\Delta \times \Delta\)-topos).
In practice, we will encounter \(D\)-topos thanks to the considerations following:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (1.2.2)

Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a fibered and co-fibered category above \(D\). We will say that \(\mathscr{E}\) is bifibered into duals of topos above \(D\) if \(\mathscr{E}^{o}\) is a \(D^{o}\)-topos.
Explicitly, \(\mathscr{E}\) is bifibered into duals of topos above \(D\) if and only if the following conditions are met:

1. (a)

    For any object \(i\) of \(D\), the dual category of the fiber category \(\mathscr{E}_i\) is a topos.

  2. (b)

    For any morphism \(m : i \to j\) of \(D\), the functor \(m^*\) is exact at right.
(1.2.4) The reader will find in paragraph 4 examples of bifibred categories in dual topos.

(1.2.5) Let \(\mathscr{E} \to B\) be a category bifibered into duals of topos above \(B\) and \(X : D^{o} \to B\) a functor. So we leave it to the reader to verify that \((D^{o} \times_B \mathscr{E})^{o}\) is a \(D\)-topos which we will note \(\overline{X}\).

(1.2.6) We will often say that a functor \(X : D^{o} \to B\) is a \(D\)-object of \(B\) and we will denote by \(X_i\) the image by this functor of an object \(i\) of \(D\); the \(D\)-objects of \(B\) form a category denoted \(D^{o}B\). If \(S\) is an object of \(B\), a \(D\)-object of \(B/S\) will be called a \(D\)-object of \(B\) augmented to \(S\). The data of a \(D\)-object of \(B\) increased to \(S\) is trivially equivalent to the given of a functional morphism \(X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) where \(X\) is a \(D\)-object of \(B\) and \(C_S^D\) the \(D\)-object of \(B\) constant with value \(S\).
When \(D = \Delta\), we will speak of simplicial object (resp. simplicial object increased).
We will also use double simplicial objects (by making \(D = \Delta \times \Delta\)).
(1.2.7) Now suppose that the category \(B\) has finished fiber products.

Let \(f : R \to S\) be an arrow in \(B\): the bifunctor \[ ([n], X) \longmapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ens}}([n], \mathrm{Hom}_S(X, R)) \] from \(\Delta^{o} \times (B/S)^{o}\) into \(\mathrm{Ens}\) defines a functor: \[ [n] \longmapsto X_{[n]} = X_n \] by taking for \(X_n\) a representative of the functor \[ Z \longmapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ens}}([n], \mathrm{Hom}_S(Z, R)) \] \[ \Big(X_n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \overbrace{R \times_S R \times_S \dots \times_S R}^{n+1 \text{ fois}}\Big) . \] We will designate by \([R\,|_f\,S]\) or \([R\,|\,S]\) the augmented semi-simplicial object to \(S\) thus constructed.

Finally if \(X\) and \(X'\) are two semi-simplicial objects of \(B\) (or of \(B/S\)) and \(u : X \to X'\) a functorial morphism. We will introduce for reasons techniques the object \([X\,|_u\,X']\) calculated in the category \(\Delta^{o}B\). This one is interpreted as a double simplicial object of \(B\) which we will then note \([[X\,|_u\,X']]\): We indeed have a canonical isomorphism of categories: \[ \Delta^{o}(\Delta^{o}B) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\Delta \times \Delta)^{o}B . \] We will now return to the general notion of \(D\)-topos.

(1.2.8) Let \(E\) be a \(D\)-topos: we will designate by \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\) the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_D(D, E)\). Let \(f : D' \to D\) be a functor: the category \(D' \times_D E\) is a \(D'\)-topos and, by composition with \(f\), we obtain a functor \[ f^* : \underline{\Gamma}(E) \longrightarrow \underline{\Gamma}(D' \times_D E) . \]

In the case where \(D'\) is reduced to an object \(i\) of \(D\) (with only morphism the identity of \(i\)) and for \(f\) the canonical inclusion denoted \(e_i\), we can take for \(D' \times_D E\) the fiber category \(E_i\) and \(e_i^*\) is then identified with functor "evaluation in \(i\)".

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (1.2.9)

If \(D'\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category, the functor \(f^*\) has an assistant on the right and on the left (noted respectively \(f_*\) and \(f_!\)).
This results from a slight generalization of Kan's lemma [III 1.1], which we will make constant use of.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (1.2.10)

Let \(I\), \(J\) and \(A\) be three categories above the same category \(B\): we assume that \(I\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small and that \(A\) is fibered and cofibered above by \(B\). We give ourselves a \(B\)-functor \(f : I \to J\) and we denote by \(f^*\) the functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(J, A) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(I, A) \] defined by composition with \(f\). Then if in each fiber of \(A\) above \(B\), the \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive (resp. projective) limits exist, \(f^*\) has an adjoint on the left (resp. right).

Proof. We will only indicate the proof of the existence of the adjoint functor on the left, the resp. from the lemma deduced by duality. We will use the following fact, the verification of which is left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (1.2.10.1)

Let \(A\) be a bifibered category above a category \(B\), \(b\) an object of \(B\) and \((F_\lambda)_{\lambda \in \Lambda}\) a functor of a category \(\Lambda\) in the fiber \(A_b\); whatever \(G\) in \(A_{b'}\), and \(u : b' \to b\) (resp. \(u : b \to b'\)) in \(B\), we have a bijection: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_u(G, \varprojlim_\lambda F_\lambda) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_\lambda \mathrm{Hom}_u(G, F_\lambda) \] (resp. \(\mathrm{Hom}_u(\varinjlim_\lambda F_\lambda, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_\lambda \mathrm{Hom}_u(F_\lambda, G)\)) whenever the first member is defined.

+ This being said, let \(I \amalg_f J\) be the category on \(B\) defined by: \[ \mathrm{ob}(I \amalg_f J) = \mathrm{ob}(I) \amalg \mathrm{ob}(J) \] \[ \mathrm{Hom}(x,y) = \begin{cases} \mathrm{Hom}_I(x,y) & \text{si } x, y \in \mathrm{ob}(I) \\ \mathrm{Hom}_J(x,y) & \text{si } x, y \in \mathrm{ob}(J) \\ \mathrm{Hom}_J(f(x), y) & \text{si } x \in \mathrm{ob}(I) \text{ et } y \in \mathrm{ob}(J) \\ \varnothing & \text{si } x \in \mathrm{ob}(J) \text{ et } y \in \mathrm{ob}(I) \end{cases} \]

+ The \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(I \amalg_f J, A)\) category is equivalent to the triple category formed of a \(B\)-functor \(F\) of \(I\) in \(A\), of a \(B\)-functor \(G\) of \(J\) in \(A\) and a morphism \(\varphi\) of \(B\)-functor of \(F\) in \(G \circ f\).

+ For any object \(j\) of \(J\), we designate by \(I/j\) the category of objects of \(I\) "placed by \(f\) above \(j\)": the objects of \(I/j\) are the couples \((i, \alpha)\) where \(i\) is an object of \(I\) and \(\alpha : i \to j\) is an arrow \(I \amalg_f J\), the morphisms of \(I/j\) being those of \(I \amalg_f J/j\). If \(p_I\) and \(p_J\) are the projections of \(I\) and \(J\) on \(B\), and if \((i, \alpha)\) is an object of \(I/j\), we will designate by \(\alpha_* : A_{p_I(i)} \to A_{p_J(j)}\) the functor \(p_J(\alpha)_*\).

+ Giving oneself a \(B\)-functor of \(I \amalg_f J\) in \(A\) again amounts to giving oneself \(F \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(I, A)\), \(G \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(J, A)\) and a functorial morphism to \(j\) \[ \psi_j : \varinjlim_{(i,\alpha) \in I/j} \alpha_*(F(i)) \longrightarrow G(j) . \] (The functoriality in \(j\) of the left member results from (1.2.10.1)). The deputy to left of \(f^*\) is therefore given by the formula: \[ f_!(F)(j) = \varinjlim_{(i,\alpha) \in I/j} \alpha_*(F(i)) \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (1.2.11)

Let \(E\) be a \(D\)-topos and \(i\) be an object of \(D\); the functor \(e_i^*\) admits an adjoint on the left (resp. on the right defined by: \[ e_{i!}(a)(j) = \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{Hom}(i,j)} \alpha_*(a) \qquad \Big(\text{resp. } e_{i*}(a)(j) = \prod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{Hom}(j,i)} \alpha^*(a)\Big) \] where \(a\) is an object of \(E\) above \(i\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (1.2.12)

Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category and \(E\) be a \(D\)-topos: then category \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos.
+ We check "fiber by fiber", using (1.2.10.1), that the category \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\) has the following properties:

1. a)

    Finite projective limits are representable.

  2. b)

    Direct sums indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) are representable. They are disjointed and universal.

  3. c)

    Equivalence relations are effectively universal.

+ It remains to show that \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\) has a system of indexed generators by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\): or, if for any object \(i\) of \(D\), \((G_{i\lambda})_{\lambda \in \Lambda_i}\) is a system of generators of \(E_i\) (where \(\Lambda_i\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small set), the family \((e_{i!}(G_{i\lambda}))_{i,\lambda}\) is a system of generators of \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\).

(1.2.13) We will now introduce the notion of morphism between \(D\)-topos. Let us first clarify that if \(F\) and \(F'\) are two categories above one same category \(B\) and if \(T : F \to F'\) is a \(B\)-functor, a \(B\)-adjoint to left to \(T\) will be a functor \(S : F' \to F\) adjoint to the left of \(T\) such that the canonical morphisms \(1 \to T \circ S\) and \(S \circ T \to 1\) are \(B\)-morphisms of functors. Under these conditions, we trivially verify that \(T\) is Cartesian and that \(S\) is Cockartesian.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (1.2.14)

Let \(E\) and \(E'\) be two \(D\)-topos: a morphism of \(E\) into \(E'\) is a pair of \(D\)-functors \(\Phi_* : E \to E'\) and \(\Phi^* : E' \to E\), provided with a \(D\)-addition \(\mathrm{Hom}(\Phi^*., .) \overset{\xi}{\underset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}} (., \Phi_*.)\), such that for any object \(i\) of \(D\), the pair \((\Phi_{*i}, \Phi_i^*)\), provided with the addition induced by \(\xi\), i.e. a topos morphism of \(E_i\) in \(E'_i\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (1.2.15)

Let \(E\) and \(E'\) be two \(D\)-topos and \((\Phi_*, \Phi^*) : E \to E'\) a morphism. We suppose that \(D\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category, then the couple \((\underline{\Gamma}(\Phi_*), \underline{\Gamma}(\Phi^*)) : \underline{\Gamma}(E) \to \underline{\Gamma}(E')\) is a topos morphism.
Follows trivially from the previous definition.
The following lemma, the proof of which is left to the reader, allows to construct morphisms of \(D\)-topos:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (1.2.16)

Let \(E\) and \(E'\) be two categories bifibered above the same category \(D\), and \(\Phi\) a \(D\)-Cartesian functor of \(E\) in \(E'\) such that, for all object \(i\) of \(D\), \(\Phi_i : E_i \to E'_i\) has a left adjoint. So, the choice for all \(i\), from a left adjoint to \(\Phi_i\) canonically determines a \(D\)-functor \(\psi : E' \to E\), \(D\)-left deputy to \(\Phi\).

blocks.title: Scholia
blocks.html:
Scholia (1.2.17)

Under the conditions of (1.2.16), suppose that \(E\) and \(E'\) are two \(D\)-topos and that for any \(i\) object of \(D\), any adjoint to the left of the functor \(\Phi_i\) is left exact: then, if \(\psi : E' \to E\) is a \(D\)-left adjoint to \(\Phi\), the pair \((\Phi, \psi) : E \to E'\) is a morphism of \(D\)-topos.

Let us now be \(\mathscr{E} \to B\) a category bifibered into duals of topos above \(B\), \(X\) and \(X'\) two \(D\)-objects of \(B\) and \(\alpha : X \to X'\) a morphism functional. Then the choice of normalized cleavages for \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{E}^{o}\) determines canonically a morphism \((\alpha_*, \alpha^*) : \overline{X} \to \overline{X}'\) of \(D\)-topos such that, for all object \(i\) of \(D\) \((\alpha_{*i}, \alpha_i^*) : \overline{X}_i \to \overline{X}'_i\) is equal to \((\alpha_{i*}, \alpha_i^*)\), where \(\alpha_i : X_i \to X'_i\) is the arrow of \(B\) given by \(\alpha\). For two different choices cleavages for \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{E}^{o}\), there exists a unique \(D\)-isomorphism between the morphisms thus obtained. (For verification of these facts, the reader can refer to \([[4] - (1.17)]\)).
1.3 Ringed D-topos

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (1.3.1)

A ringed \(D\)-topos is a pair \((E, A)\) where \(E\) is a \(D\)-topos and \(A\) a ring of \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\).
We then check that for any object \(i\) of \(D\), \(A_i\) is a ring of the topos \(E_i\) and that for any morphism \(m : i \to j\) the canonical arrow \(A_i \to m^*(A_j)\) is a ring homomorphism.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (1.3.2)

Let \((E, A)\) and \((E', A')\) be two \(D\)-ringed topos: a morphism of \((E, A)\) in \((E', A')\) is a pair \((\Phi, \theta)\) where \(\Phi : E \to E'\) is a morphism of \(D\)-topos and \(\theta : A' \to \underline{\Gamma}(\Phi_*)(A)\) is a ring homomorphism.

A morphism \(\Phi : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) of \(D\)-ringed topos induced a morphism \((\underline{\Gamma}(\Phi), \theta) : (\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \to (\underline{\Gamma}(E'), A')\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed topos when \(D\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category (see 1.2.15).

(1.3.4) Let \(\mathscr{E} \to B\) be a category bifibered into duals of topos above \(B\) and \(\mathfrak{G}\) a ring of \(\underline{\Gamma}(\mathscr{E}^{o}) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{B^{o}}(B^{o}, \mathscr{E}^{o})\). If \(X : D^{o} \to B\) is a \(D\)-object of \(B\), the \(D\)-topos \(\overline{X}\) (cf. (1.2.5)) is naturally annealed by \((\mathfrak{G} \cdot X)^{o} : D \to \mathscr{E}\) and we will designate by \((\overline{X}, \mathfrak{G})\) the \(D\)-ringed topos thus constructed. If \(\alpha : X \to X'\) is a functional morphism, the morphism \((\alpha_*, \alpha^*) : \overline{X} \to \overline{X}'\) (cf. 1.2.17) induces canonically a morphism \((\overline{X}, \mathfrak{G}) \to (\overline{X}', \mathfrak{G})\) of \(D\)-ringed topos still denoted \(\alpha\).

(1.3.5) A ringed \(D\)-topos \((E, A)\) canonically defines a category \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A)\) bifibrated in abelian category above \(D\) whose fiber in an object \(i\) of \(D\) is the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E_i, A_i)\) of modules on the ringed topos \((E_i, A_i)\). With these notations, the module category of \(\underline{\Gamma}(E)\) on \(A\), denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) identifies with the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_D(D, \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A))\).

(1.3.6) Let \(\varphi = (\Phi, \theta) : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) be a morphism of \(D\)-ringed topos. He defines two functors \(\varphi_* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A) \to \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E', A')\) and \(\varphi^* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E', A') \to \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A)\) between the corresponding module categories:

- Let \(M\) be an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A)\) above an object \(i\) of \(D\): \(\Phi_*(M)\) is a module on \(\Phi_*(A_i)\) and, thanks to the morphism \(\theta_i : A'_i \to \Phi_*(A_i)\), we deduces a module on \(A'_i\) denoted \(\varphi_*(M)\). This functor \(\varphi_*\) will be called the functor direct image by morphism \(\varphi\).

- Let \(M'\) be an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E', A')\) above an object \(i\) of \(D\): \(\Phi^*(M')\) is a module on \(\Phi^*(A'_i)\) and \(\varphi^*(M') = \Phi^*(M') \otimes_{\Phi^*(A'_i)} A_i\) is canonically equipped of a module structure on \(A_i\). By means of (1.2.16), we thus define a functor \(\varphi^*\), left adjoint to \(\varphi_*\), and called reciprocal image functor by the morphism \(\varphi\).
(1.3.6.1) We will say that \(\varphi\) is flat if the functor \(\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi^*)\) is exact.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (1.3.7)

Let \(f : D' \to D\) be a functor and \((E, A)\) be a ringed \(D\)-topos. Then the canonical functor \[ f^* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E \times_D D'), A \circ f) \] has a right and left adjoint if \(D'\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category. In particular, it is correct.

This follows immediately from (1.2.10) and the identification \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_D(D, \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\).
In accordance with general notations, we will note \(f_!\) (resp. \(f_*\)) the deputy on the left (resp. right) of \(f^*\).

(1.3.8) The following considerations provide us with a convenient calculation process for derived functors of type \(\mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*) : \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \to \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E'), A')\), where \(\varphi : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) is a morphism of \(D\)-ringed topos (cf. (1.3.6)). [If \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) is a ringed topos, we note \(\mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) the category derived from the category \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules of \(S\)].
(1.3.9) This formal calculation could also be applied to other contexts such as that the "\(\ell\)-adic cohomology descent" (cf. S.G.A. 5).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (1.3.10)

Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category. If \((E, A)\) is a \(D\)-topos ringed, we designate by \(I_{(E,A)}\) the set of objects of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) isomorphic to an object of the form \(\prod_{i \in \mathrm{ob}(D)} e_{i*}(Q_i)\), where, for any object \(i\) of \(D\), \(Q_i\) is totally acyclic (cf. V 4.1)(∗); \(I_{(E,A)}\) checks the following properties:

  1. (i)

    For any object \(F\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) and any object \(i\) of \(D\), \(e_i^*(F)\) is totally acyclic.

  2. (ii)

    Any object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) injects into an object of \(I_{(E,A)}\).

  3. (iii)

    \(I_{(E,A)}\) is stable under finite direct sums.

  4. (iv)

    For any morphism \(\varphi : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) of \(D\)-ringed topos, the functor \(\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)\) transforms any acyclic complex of \(C^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\), formed from objects of \(I_{(E,A)}\), into an acyclic complex formed of objects of \(I_{(E',A')}\).
Demonstration.
(i) Given the explicit expression of \(e_{i*}\) (cf. (1.2.11)), it suffices to show the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (1.3.10.1)

Let \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be a ringed topos and \((F_t)_{t \in T}\) be a family of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-fully acyclic modules indexed by a set \(T\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. So \(\prod_{t \in T} F_t\) is completely acyclic.

Let \(X\) be an object of \(S\): there exists a spectral sequence \[ (1.3.10.2) \qquad H^p\Big(X, \prod_{t \in T}^{(q)} F_t\Big) \Longrightarrow \prod_{t \in T} H^{p+q}(X, F_t) \] where \(\prod_{t \in T}^{(q)}\) designates the \(q\)-th derivative of the functor "product indexed by \(T\)". Like \(\prod_{t \in T}^{(q)} F_t\) is the beam associated with pre-beam \(U \longmapsto \prod_{t \in T} H^q(U, F_t)\), (1.3.10.2) degenerates and we obtain isomorphisms \[ (1.3.10.3) \qquad H^n\Big(X, \prod_{t \in T} F_t\Big) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \prod_{t \in T} H^n(X, F_t) \] hence finally \(H^n(X, \prod_{t \in T}) = 0\) for all \(n\).

(ii) Let \(F\) be an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\); we choose for everything \(i\) a monomorphism \(F_i \to Q_i\), where \(Q_i\) is completely acyclic in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E_i, A_i)\): \(e_{i*}(F_i) \to e_{i*}(Q_i)\) is then a monomorphism (since \(e_{i*}\) has an assistant on the left), and the canonical arrow: \[ (1.3.10.4) \qquad F \longrightarrow \prod_{i \in \mathrm{ob}(D)} e_{i*}(F_i) \longrightarrow \prod_{i \in \mathrm{ob}(D)} e_{i*}(Q_i) \] is still a monomorphism, as we verify trivially.
(iii) Demonstration left to the reader.

(iv) We also leave it to the reader to verify that \(\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)\) transforms a object of \(I_{(E,A)}\) into an object of \(I_{(E',A')}\). This established point, it is enough, counts given (i), to verify the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (1.3.10.5)

If \(0 \to F \to G \to H \to 0\) is a short exact sequence in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\), with \(F \in I_{(E,A)}\) and \(G \in I_{(E,A)}\), the sequel \(0 \to \underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)(F) \to \underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)(G) \to \underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)(H) \to 0\) is exact in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E'), A')\).

Just note that \(e_i^*(H)\) is acyclic for any object \(i\) of \(D\) and that the calculation of \(\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)\) is done fiber by fiber, which completes the demonstration from (1.3.10).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (1.3.11)

Let \(\varphi : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) be a morphism of \(D\)-ringed topos. We can calculate the functor \(\mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)\) using resolutions formed from objects from \(I_{(E,A)}\).

Let \(L \subset K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) be the full subcategory of closed complexes of objects of \(I_{(E,A)}\): \(L\) is a triangulated subcategory under ((1.3.10), (ii)) and we can apply theorem (5.1) of \([[7] - \text{Chap.1}]\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (1.3.12)

Let \(\varphi : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) be a morphism of \(D\)-ringed topos; for any object \(i\) of \(D\) we denote by \(\varphi_i : (E_i, A_i) \to (E'_i, A'_i)\) the morphism of \(D\)-ringed topos induced by \(\varphi\) above \(i\). So the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{R}^+(e_i^*)} \ar[d]_{\mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)} & & \mathrm{D}^+(E_i, A_i) \ar[d]^{\mathrm{R}^+\varphi_{i*}} \\ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E'), A') \ar[rr]_-{\mathrm{R}^+(e_i^*)} & & \mathrm{D}^+(E'_i, A'_i) } \] is essentially commutative.

We have a morphism \[ \mathrm{R}^+(e_i^*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*) \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}^+\varphi_{i*} \circ \mathrm{R}^+(e_i^*) \] which we verify that it is an isomorphism, thanks to (1.3.10).

Let us designate by \(G_{(E,A)}\) all the objects \(F\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) such that \(e_i^*(F)\) is totally acyclic for any object \(i\) of \(D\). It results from the demonstration of (1.3.10) that one can calculate \(\mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\varphi_*)\) by means of resolutions formed from objects of \(G_{(E,A)}\); This is what we do in particular in the introduction using the "canonical flaccid resolution"(∗). According to \([(1.3.10)(i)]\), we have \(I_{(E,A)} \subset G_{(E,A)}\), but we will explicitly use \(I_{(E,A)}\) in paragraph 2.

footnotes.html: (∗) cf. presentation XVII for the generalization of this notion.

blocks.html: 2. The method of cohomological descent
In this issue, \(D\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category.
2.1 General. Ratings

(2.1.1) Let \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be a ringed topos. The category \(S \times D\), provided with the projection canonical \(S \times D \to D\), is a \(D\)-topos; plus the constant value section \(\mathcal{O}_S\) defines a \(D\)-ringed topos \((S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S)\) called \(D\)-topos ringed constant value \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\). With these notations, the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S)\) identifies with the category covariant functors of \(D\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\).

We define an exact functor: \[ \varepsilon^* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \] by associating with any module \(F\) on \(S\) the constant functor with value \(F\). The functor \(\varepsilon^*\) has a right adjoint \(\varepsilon_*\) which associates with any functor \(H : D \to \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathcal{O}_S)\) its projective limit, the adjunction morphism \(F \to \varepsilon_* \varepsilon^*(F)\) being the one which sends \(F\) in the projective limit of the constant value functor \(F\); thus \(\varepsilon^*\) is fully faithful if and only if \(D\) is connected.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.1.2)

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed \(D\)-topos; an increase of \((E, A)\) is a morphism (from \(D\)-ringed topos) of \((E, A)\) into a constant \(D\)-ringed topos. A \(D\)-ringed topos with an augmentation will be called an \(D\)-augmented ringed topos.

(2.1.3) Let \(\mathscr{E} \to B\) be a category bifibered into duals of topos and \(\mathfrak{G}\) be a ring from \(\underline{\Gamma}(\mathscr{E}^{o}) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{B^{o}}(B^{o}, \mathscr{E}^{o})\). Let \(S\) be an object of \(B\): a functor \(D^{o} \to B/S\), that is, a functorial morphism \(X \xrightarrow{\ \theta\ } C_S^D\), where \(X\) is a functor \(D^{o} \to B\) (cf. (1.2.6)), induces an increase: \[ \theta : (\overline{X}, \mathfrak{G}) \longrightarrow (\mathscr{E}_S^{o} \times D, \mathfrak{G}_S) \qquad \text{(cf. (1.3.4))}, \] which we will designate by the same letter as the functional morphism which gives it birth.
2.2 Cohomological descent

(2.2.1) Let \(\theta : (E, A) \longrightarrow (S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be an augmented \(D\)-topos, we set, with the notations of (1.3.6) and (2.1.1), \[ \overline{\theta}^* = \underline{\Gamma}(\theta^*) \circ \varepsilon^* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \] and \[ \overline{\theta}_* = \varepsilon_* \circ \underline{\Gamma}(\theta_*) : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S) . \] The image of \(\overline{\theta}^*\) is in the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) formed from the Cockartesian sections of \(\underline{\Gamma}(D, \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A))\): we will note \(\underline{\Gamma}^{\mathrm{cocart}}(D, \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A))\) this last category.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.2.2)

We say that \(\theta\) is an effective descent increase if \(\underline{\Gamma}(\theta^*) \circ \varepsilon^* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow \underline{\Gamma}^{\mathrm{cocart}}(D, \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A))\) is an equivalence of categories.

(2.2.3) With the notations of (2.2.1), suppose that \(\theta\) is flat, so that \(\overline{\theta}^*\) is exact and passes trivially to derived categories: let \(\mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*)\) be functor thus obtained. With these notations:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (2.2.3.1)

There are two functorial morphisms \[ \alpha : \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*) \circ \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*) \quad \text{et} \quad \beta : \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*) \longrightarrow \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)} \] putting these two functors in addition.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.2.4)

We say that \(\theta\) is a cohomological 1-descent increase if

  1. 1°)

    \(\theta\) is flat

  2. 2°)

    The functor \(\mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*)\) is fully faithful.
Condition 2°) the previous definition can also be expressed by saying that the morphism \(\alpha\) in (2.2.3.1) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.2.6)

We say that \(\theta\) is a cohomological 2-descent increase (or effective cohomological descent) if \(\theta\) is both an increase of effective descent and a cohomological 1-descent increase.
The previous terminology is justified by the following result:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.2.7)

Let \(\theta\) be an effective cohomological descent increase. Then, the essential image of \(\mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*)\) is the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\) formed of complexes \(F'\) such that for all \(i\), \(H^i(F')\) is a cocartesian section by \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(E, A)\).

We will say, for brevity, that a complex \(F'\) checking the conditions previous is cohomological descent data. It is clear that if \(K' \in \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) \(\mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*)(K')\) is a cohomological descent data: it It is therefore sufficient to show that for any cohomological descent data \(F'\), the canonical morphism \[ \beta(F') : \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(F') \longrightarrow F' \] is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A)\).
a) Case where \(F'\) is bounded: we reason by induction on the length \(\ell\) of the range of integers \(i\) where \(H^i(F') \neq 0\):
- for \(\ell \leq 1\) the assertion is true because \(\theta\) is of effective descent.

- suppose \(\ell > 1\) and let \(n\) be the largest integer such that \(H^n(F') \neq 0\): we have a distinguished triangle(∗) \[ \xymatrix@C=1em@R=2.5em{ & H^n(F')[-n] \ar[dl] & \\ F'' \ar[rr] & & F' \ar[ul] } \] such that the recurrence hypothesis applies to \(F''\); \(\beta(H^n(F')[-n])\) and \(\beta(F'')\) being isomorphisms, the same is true of \(\beta(F')\).

b) General case: let us designate by \(\sigma_{\leq n}(F')\) the complex \[ \cdots \longrightarrow F^{n-2} \longrightarrow F^{n-1} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ker} d^n \longrightarrow 0 \longrightarrow 0 \longrightarrow \cdots , \] (cf. [7] (7.1)), so that we have for all \(n\) a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(\sigma_{\leq n}(F')) \ar[rr]^-{\sim} \ar[d] & & \sigma_{\leq n}(F') \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(F') \ar[rr] & & F' } \] and it suffices to see that the morphism \[ \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(\sigma_{\leq n}(F')) \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(F') \] induces an isomorphism on the \(H^i\) for \(i \leq n\). Now, we have a triangle: \[ \xymatrix@C=1em@R=2.5em{ & F''' \ar[dl] & \\ \sigma_{\leq n}(F') \ar[rr] & & F' \ar[ul] } \] with \(H^i(F''') = 0\) for \(i \leq n\). We then deduce that \(H^i(\mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(F''')) = 0\) for \(i \leq n\), then \(H^i(\mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(\sigma_{\leq n}(F'))) \longrightarrow H^i(\mathrm{R}^+(\overline{\theta}_*)(F'))\) is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n\), which completes the demonstration.

We will now present an explicit method for calculating \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_*\) very useful in the demonstration of certain criteria of cohomological descent, as well as in the exploitation of the said notion.
2.3 A calculation process for \(\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_*\)
We will start with two exits.

(2.3.1) Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category and \(\mathrm{Ab}\) be the category of groups abelians belonging to \(\mathcal{U}\). We denote by \(r_D\) the functor \(D \to (\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab}))^{o}\) defined by the relationship \[ r_D(i)(j) = \mathbf{Z}^{\mathrm{Hom}(i,j)} \] for any pair \((i, j)\) of objects of \(D\). We then construct, for any couple \((i, X)\) formed from an object of \(D\) and an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab})\), a canonical isomorphism and functional: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(r_D(i), X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X(i) \]

(2.3.1.1) Let \(A\) be an additive category (cf. Tohoku) and \(F\) be a functor \(D \to A\): we define a functor \(\overline{F} : (\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab}))^{o} \longrightarrow A^{o}\mathrm{Ens}\) by the relation \[ \overline{F}(X)(a) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab})}(X, \mathrm{Hom}_A(a, F(.))) \] so that the following diagram, where \(h_A\) denotes the canonical functor: \[ \xymatrix{ D \ar[rr]^-{r_D} \ar[d]_{F} & & (\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab}))^{o} \ar[d]^{\overline{F}} \\ A \ar[rr]_-{h_A} & & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(A^{o}, \mathrm{Ens}) } \] is essentially commutative.

We leave it to the reader to verify that \(\overline{F}\) transforms the sums finite direct connections of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab})\) into products and that, if we designate by \(\mathbf{Z}\) the constant functor \(D \to \mathrm{Ab}\) with value \(\mathbf{Z}\), we have \(\overline{F}(\mathbf{Z}) = \varprojlim F\).

Finally the correspondence \(F \rightsquigarrow \overline{F}\) defines a covariant functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, A) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}((\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab}))^{o}, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(A^{o}, \mathrm{Ens})) . \]

(2.3.1.2) We denote by \(\mathrm{Add}(D)\) the full subcategory of \((\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab}))^{o}\) defined by objects of the form \(r_D(i)\), where \(i\) is an object of \(D\), and their finite direct sums. The category \(\mathrm{Add}(D)\) is additive and the functor \(r_D : D \to \mathrm{Add}(D)\) checks for the following universal property:

(2.3.1.3) For any additive category \(A\), the functor \(G \longmapsto G \circ r_D\) induces an equivalence of the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Add}(D), A)\) formed by the additive functors on the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, A)\).
The category \(\mathrm{Add}(D)\), defined to the nearest equivalence by (2.3.1.3) will be called the additive category generated by \(D\).

(2.3.2) Let \(A\) be an abelian category and \(K''\) be a double complex that we let us consider as an object of \(C^+(C^+(A))\), the first index corresponding to the first sign \(C^+\): we designate by \((K'')_s\) the associated simple complex (cf. Tohoku (2.4), for which we will keep the notations). We thus define a functor \[ (2.3.2.1) \qquad (\ )_s : C^+(C^+(A)) \longrightarrow C^+(A) \] and we leave it to the reader to verify the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (2.3.2.2)

The functor \((\ )_s\) defines a triangulated functor: \[ K^+(C^+(A)) \longrightarrow K^+(A) \] which preserves quasi-isomorphisms; it therefore defines a triangulated functor: \[ \mathrm{D}^+(C^+(A)) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^+(A) , \] noted again \((\ )_s\).
Remark. We have the same result for the functor \((\ )_s : C(C^b(A)) \to C(A)\) because the spectral sequence that we are considering is biregular by (E.G.A. III (11.3.3)).

(2.3.3) This being said, let \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be a ringed topos and \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category. Let \(Z' \in C^+(\mathrm{Add}(D))\) be a cochain complex such that \(Z^n = 0\) for \(n < 0\).

Any object \(F\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S), \mathcal{O}_S)\) (which identifies with a functor \(D \to \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) according to (2.1.1)) defined, by the universal property of \(\mathrm{Add}(D)\) an object \(\varepsilon_{Z'*}(F)\) of \(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) which varies functionally with \(F\) according to (2.3.1.3).

We thus define a triangulated functor: \[ (2.3.3.1) \qquad K^+(\varepsilon_{Z'*}) : K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow K^+(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) . \] Furthermore, it follows from (2.3.2.2) that the compound functor: \[ (\ )_s \circ K^+(\varepsilon_{Z'*}) : K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow K^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] transforms acyclic objects into acyclic objects. It therefore defines a functor triangulated: \[ (2.3.3.2) \qquad \mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} : \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] checking the relation \(Q \circ (\ )_s \circ K^+(\varepsilon_{Z'*}) = \mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} \circ Q\)(∗).

(2.3.4) Previous data is retained. We consider \(Z'\) as a string complex in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D, \mathrm{Ab})\). Let \(t : Z^{0} \to \mathbf{Z}\) be a morphism of \(Z^{0}\) in the constant value functor \(\mathbf{Z}\) such as the compound morphism \[ (2.3.4.1) \qquad Z^1 \longrightarrow Z^{0} \xrightarrow{\ t\ } \mathbf{Z} \] is zero.

By functoriality (cf. (2.3.1.1)), we deduce a morphism \(\varepsilon_*(F) \to \varepsilon_{Z'*}(F)^{0}\) such as the compound morphism \[ (2.3.4.2) \qquad \varepsilon_*(F) \longrightarrow \varepsilon_{Z'*}(F)^{0} \longrightarrow \varepsilon_{Z'*}(F)^{1} \] is zero.

There then exists a canonical morphism of triangulated functors: \[ (2.3.4.3) \qquad Q \circ K^+(\varepsilon_*) \xrightarrow{\ j\ } Q \circ (\ )_s \circ K^+(\varepsilon_{Z'*}) = \mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} \circ Q \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.3.5)

The conditions and notations of (2.3.3) and (2.3.4) are retained; we further assume that the augmented complex \[ Z^n \longrightarrow Z^{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots \longrightarrow Z^1 \longrightarrow Z^{0} \xrightarrow{\ t\ } \mathbf{Z} \] sets a resolution of \(\mathbf{Z}\).

Then, for any complex \(F'\) formed of objects of \(I_{(S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S)}\) (cf. 1.3.10), \[ j(F') : Q \circ K^+(\varepsilon_*)(F') \longrightarrow (\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} \circ Q)(F') \] is an isomorphism.

+ Let \(i\) be an object of \(D\) and \(Q_i\) be a module on \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\): let us show that \(j(e_{i*}(Q_i))\) is an isomorphism. The point is to see that the following: \[ (2.3.5.1) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow Q_i \longrightarrow \varepsilon_{Z'*}(e_{i*}(Q_i))^{0} \longrightarrow \varepsilon_{Z'*}(e_{i*}(Q_i))^{1} \longrightarrow \cdots \] is correct.

For this it suffices to show that, for any object \(X\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\), the following: \[ (2.3.5.2) \quad 0 \to \mathrm{Hom}(X, Q_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X, \varepsilon_{Z'*}(e_{i*}(Q_i))^{0}) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X, \varepsilon_{Z'*}(e_{i*}(Q_i))^{1}) \to \cdots \] is correct. But an immediate calculation shows that (2.3.5.2) reduces to: \[ (2.3.5.3) \quad 0 \to \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbf{Z}, \mathrm{Hom}(X, Q_i)) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Add}(D)}(Z^{0}, i), \mathrm{Hom}(X, Q_i)) \to \] \[ \to \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Add}(D)}(Z^{1}, i), \mathrm{Hom}(X, Q_i)) \to \cdots . \]

+ If for any object \(i\) of \(D\), \(Q_i\) is a totally acyclic module, we sees, by applying the functor \(\prod_{i \in \mathrm{ob}(D)}\) to the complexes (2.3.5.1), that we again obtains an acyclic complex according to (1.3.10.1). So \(j(\prod_{i \in \mathrm{ob}(D)} e_{i*}(Q_i))\) is an isomorphism.
+ We leave it to the reader to deduce from this that \(j(F')\) is a isomorphism when \(F^n \in I_{(S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S)}\) for all \(n\) (by spectral sequences, by example).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (2.3.6)

Under the conditions of (2.3.5), the morphism \(j\) defines \(\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*}\) as the functor derived from \(\varepsilon_*\).

The subcategory of \(K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S)\) defined by the complexes formed of objects of \(I_{(S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S)}\) verifies the conditions of theorem (5.1) of \([[7], I]\), for the functor \(K^+(\varepsilon_*)\), by virtue of (1.3.10) and (2.3.5): the corollary results immediately from this remark.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (2.3.7)

Under the conditions of (2.3.5), let \((E, A)\) be a \(D\)-ringed topos and \(\theta : (E, A) \to (S \times D, \mathcal{O}_S)\) an increase; there exists a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\theta_*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} . \]
This results from \([[7] - I - 5.4]\).

We knew a priori that \(\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_*\) exists and that we have an isomorphism \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\theta_*) \circ \mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_*\), since \(\underline{\Gamma}(\theta_*)\) and \(\varepsilon_*\) are induced by morphisms of ringed topos (cf. (1.3.6) and (2.1.1)). However the previous calculation will prove very useful and applicable to other contexts, because, in practice, the conditions of (2.3.5) will always be verified.
We will now give the fundamental examples where we can apply (2.3.5).

(2.3.9) In \(\mathrm{Add}(\Delta)\), we set \(Z^n = [n]\) and we define \(d^n : Z^n \to Z^{n+1}\) by the formula \[ (2.3.9.1) \qquad d^n = \sum_{i=0}^{n+1} (-1)^i \partial^i \] where \(\partial^i : [n] \to [n+1]\) is the \(i\)-th face [cf. [3] II 2]. We take for \(t : Z^{0} \to \mathbf{Z}\) the obvious natural increase.
The condition of (2.3.4) is trivially verified. The condition of (2.3.5) results from ([5] I (3.7.4)).

(2.3.10) In \(\mathrm{Add}(\Delta \times \Delta)\), we consider the simple complex associated with the complex double: \[ (2.3.10.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ ([n], [m+1]) \ar[rr]^-{s_1^{n,m+1}} & & ([n+1], [m+1]) \\ ([n], [m]) \ar[rr]_-{s_1^{n,m}} \ar[u]^{s_2^{n,m}} & & ([n+1], [m]) \ar[u]_{s_2^{n+1,m}} } \] with \[ (2.3.10.2) \qquad \begin{cases} s_1^{n,m} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n+1} (-1)^i (\partial^i, \mathrm{id}_{[m]}) \\[1.5ex] s_2^{n,m} = \displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{m+1} (-1)^j (\mathrm{id}_{[n]}, \partial^j) . \end{cases} \] We leave it to the reader to verify (using (2.3.9)) that the complex thus defined, provided with the obvious natural increase, verifies the conditions from (2.3.5).
(2.3.11) We treat the multi-simplicial case in an analogous manner (with \(\underbrace{\Delta \times \Delta \times \dots \times \Delta}_{p\text{-fois}}\)).
2.4 Relative cohomological descent
(2.4.0) Let \(\mathscr{E} \to B\) be a category bifibered into duals of topos and \(\mathfrak{G}\) be a ring by \(\underline{\Gamma}(\mathscr{E}^{o})\).

These data canonically define a fibered and cofibered category in abelian categories, denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G})\), above \(B^{o}\) (cf. (1.3.3) and (1.3.4)): in everything that follows, the “direct images” and “reciprocal images” functors will always be taken with respect to the fibrant and cofibrant functor: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G}) \longrightarrow B^{o} . \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.0.1)

We will say that a morphism \(f : T \to S\) in \(B\) is flat (relative to \((\mathscr{E}, \mathfrak{G})\)) if \(f_* : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_S) \to \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_T^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_T)\) is a functor correct.

(2.4.1) Let \(G\) be a fibered and cofibered subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G})\) such that, for any object \(S\) of \(B\), \(G_S\) or a thick subcategory (cf. Tohoku (1.11)) from \((\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G}))_S\) — The reader will find in paragraph 4 examples of such situations.

If \(S\) is an object of \(B\), we will designate by \(\mathrm{D}^+(S)\), the category derived from \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_S)\): we introduce, following \([[7]\ (I\ \S\ 4)]\), the category \(\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\), which is identified with the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{D}^+(S)\) formed by the objects \(X'\) such that \(H^n(X') \in G_S\) for all \(n\).
We will assume that the following condition is always true.

(2.4.1.1) For any morphism \(h : T \to S\) in \(B\), the functor \(\mathrm{R}^+(h^*) : \mathrm{D}^+(T) \to \mathrm{D}^+(S)\) is such that \(\mathrm{R}^+(h^*)(F) \in \mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\) if \(F \in G_T\).
According to \([[7]\ (I.\ (7.3).(ii))]\), this condition causes that \(\mathrm{R}^+(h^*)(\mathrm{D}_{G_T}^+(T)) \subset \mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\).

(2.4.2) Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category: we will assume in all that follows that we have given ourselves a complex \(Z'\) of \(\mathrm{Add}(D)\) verifying the conditions of (2.3.5) (in applications, we will actually have \(D = \Delta\) or \(D = \Delta \times \Delta\)).

If \(X : D^{o} \to B\) is a \(D\)-object of \(B\), we denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}_G(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) formed by the objects \(F\) such that \(e_i^*(F) \in G_i\ (= G_{X_i})\) for any object \(i\) of \(D\): \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}_G(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) is a subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) thick. We will designate by \(K_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) (resp. \(\mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\)) the full subcategory of \(K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) (resp. \(\mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\)) formed by objects \(X'\) such as \(H^n(X') \in \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}_G(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) for all \(n\).

(2.4.3) Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be two \(D\)-objects of \(B\) and \(\alpha : X \longrightarrow X'\) a morphism functional. Recall (cf. (1.3.4)) that we still note \(\alpha : (\overline{X}, \mathfrak{G}) \longrightarrow (\overline{X}', \mathfrak{G})\) the morphism of \(D\)-corresponding ringed topos.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.3.1)

We will say that \(\alpha : X \to X'\) is flat if the morphism of \(D\)-corresponding ringed topos is flat in the sense of (1.3.6.1).
We then have the obvious lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (2.4.3.2)

For \(\alpha : X \to X'\) to be flat, it is necessary and sufficient that, for any object \(i\) of \(D\), \(\alpha_i : X_i \to X'_i\) is a flat morphism (cf. (2.4.0.1)).

(2.4.4) Let \(\alpha : X \to X'\) be a morphism, it defines a functor \[ (2.4.4.1) \qquad K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*)) : K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G}) \longrightarrow K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}'), \mathfrak{G}) . \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (2.4.4.2)

The functor \[ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*)) \big|\, K_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G}) : K_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G}) \longrightarrow K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}'), \mathfrak{G}) \] has a right-derived functor, denoted \(\mathrm{R}_G^+\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*)\), and the canonical morphism \[ \mathrm{R}_G^+\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*) \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*) \big|\, \mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G}) \] is a morphism.

Additionally \(\mathrm{R}_G^+(\mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})) \subset \mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}'), \mathfrak{G})\).

The first part results from (1.3.10) and \([[7] - (I.(5.2))]\). For verify the last assertion, just show, under \([[7] - (I.7.3).(ii)]\), that \(\mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*)(F) \in \mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}'), \mathfrak{G})\) when \(F \in \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}_G(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\), which results from explicit calculation of \(\mathrm{R}^+\underline{\Gamma}(\alpha_*)\) given by (1.3.11) and the hypothesis (2.4.1.1).

(2.4.5) Let \(S\) be an object of \(B\): the \(D\)-ringed topos \((\overline{C_S^D}, \mathfrak{G})\) (cf. (1.2.6)) identifies with the constant ringed \(D\)-topos \((\mathscr{E}_S^{o} \times D, \mathfrak{G}_S)\) and we have the following lemma which results from \([[7](I.7.3).(ii))]\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (2.4.5.1)

The functor \(\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} \big|\, \mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{C_S^D}), \mathfrak{G})\), denoted \(\mathrm{R}_G^+\varepsilon_{Z'*}\), is with values in \(\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\) and identifies with a functor derived to the right of the functor \[ K^+(\varepsilon_*) \big|\, K_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{C_S^D}), \mathfrak{G}) : K_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{C_S^D}), \mathfrak{G}) \longrightarrow K^+(S) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.4.6)

Let \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) be a \(D\)-object of \(B\) augmented: the functor \(K^+(\overline{\theta}_*) \big|\, K_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) has a right-derived functor, denoted \(\mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{\theta}_*\), which takes its values in \(\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\). Furthermore, there is a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{\theta}_* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}_G^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} \circ \mathrm{R}_G^+\underline{\Gamma}(\theta_*) . \]
Demonstration left to the reader from the above.
We are now able to give the relative definitions to cohomological descent.

(2.4.7) Let \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) be an \(D\)-object of \(B\) augmented. Suppose \(\theta\) is flat, so that the restriction to \(\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\) of the functor \(\mathrm{L}^+\overline{\theta}^*\) takes its values in \(\mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\): we will note \(\mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{\theta}^*\) this restriction. With these notations:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (2.4.7.1)

There are two functorial morphisms \(\alpha : \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)} \to \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{\theta}_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{\theta}^*\) and \(\beta : \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{\theta}^* \circ \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{\theta}_* \longrightarrow \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})}\) putting these two functors in addition.
This follows immediately from (2.2.3.1) and (2.4.6).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.8)

We say that \(\theta\) is a cohomological 1-descent increase relative to \(G\) if

  1. 1°)

    \(\theta\) is flat.

  2. 2°)

    The functor \(\mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{\theta}^*\) is fully faithful.
Condition 2°) of the previous definition can still be express yourself by saying that the morphism \(\alpha\) in (2.4.7.1) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.10)

Let \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) be an \(D\)-object of \(B\) augmented. It is said that \(\theta\) is an effective descent increase relative to \(G\) if the functor \[ \underline{\Gamma}(\theta^*) \circ \varepsilon^* : G_S \longrightarrow \underline{\Gamma}^{\mathrm{Cocart}}(D, G) \] is an equivalence of categories.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.11)

We say that \(\theta\) is a cohomological 2-descent increase (or effective cohomological descent) relative to \(G\) if \(\theta\) is both a increase of cohomological 1-descent and effective descent relative to \(G\).
We then deduce from the demonstration of (2.2.7) the following result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem (2.4.12)

Let \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) be a cohomological 2-descent increase relative to \(G\). Then the essential image of \(\mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{\theta}^*\) is the subcategory full of \(\mathrm{D}_G^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathfrak{G})\) formed of complexes \(F'\) such that for all \(n\), \(H^n(F')\) i.e. a Cockartesian section of \(G\).
The following proposition, the verification of which is left to the reader, allows you to transcribe the definition (2.4.10) in the language of (\([4]\). 6):

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.4.13)

So that \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) is a descent increase effective relative to \(G\), it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \(D^{o} \longrightarrow B/S\) above \(B\), defined by \(\theta\), induce an equivalence between the category of Cartesian sections of \(G^{o}\) above \(B/S\) and the category of sections Cartesian values of \(G^{o}\) above \(D^{o}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (2.4.14)

Suppose that the finished fiber products are representable in \(B\) and be \(f : R \to S\) a morphism: so that the semi-simplicial object increased \([R\,|_f\,S]\) (cf. (1.2.7)) or effective descent relative to \(G\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) be an effective descent morphism for the fibered category \(G^{o}\) above \(B\).
This follows from (2.4.13) and (\([4] - 9\)).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.15)

Suppose that the finished fiber products are represetable in \(B\) and let \(f : R \to S\) be a morphism. We will say that \(f\) is a morphism of \(i\)-cohomological descent relative to \(G\) (\(i = 1, 2\)) if the semi-simplicial object increased \([R\,|_f\,S]\) is \(i\)-cohomological descent relative to \(G\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (2.4.16)

Suppose that the finished fiber products are representable in \(B\) and let \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) be an increase: we will say that \(\theta\) is an increase of \(i\)-universal cohomological descent (\(i = 1, 2\)) relative to \(G\) if, for any morphism \(T \longrightarrow S\), the increase \(X_T \xrightarrow{\ \theta_T\ } C_T^D\) obtained by change of base is an increase of relatively cohomological \(i\)-descent at \(G\).
The notion of morphism of \(i\)-relatively universal cohomological descent to \(G\) is immediately deduced from the two previous definitions.
We will often use the terminology "\(G\)-\(i\)-decrease cohomological" instead of "increase of \(i\)-cohomological descent relatively to \(G\)".
2.5 The spectral descent sequence

(2.5.1) Let \(A\) be an abelian category. We designate by \(F(A)\) the category of which the objects are the objects of \(A\), provided with discrete and co-discrete filtration, and whose morphisms are the filtered morphisms of \(A\): the category \(F(A)\) is an additive category (but not abelian).

The category \(K(F(A))\) of filtered complexes of \(A\), discrete filtration and codiscrete degree by degree, is a triangulated category and the functors canonical \[ (2.5.1.1) \qquad \mathrm{Gr}_n : K(F(A)) \longrightarrow K(A) \] are triangulated. The set \(\Sigma\) of morphisms \(f\) of \(K(F(A))\) such that \(\mathrm{Gr}_n(f)\) or a quasi-isomorphism for all \(n\) is therefore a saturated multiplicative system [cf. [9]. §2. n°1].

By reversing the arrows of this system, we obtain a new category triangulated denoted \(\mathrm{D}\,F(A)\) and the functors \(\mathrm{Gr}_n\) are extended into functors \[ (2.5.1.2) \qquad \mathrm{Gr}_n : \mathrm{D}\,F(A) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}(A) . \]
We will subsequently only consider lower bounded complexes: we naturally introduce the notations \(K^+(F(A))\) and \(\mathrm{D}^+F(A)\).

(2.5.2) Let \(B\) be a thick subcategory of \(A\) and denote by \(K_B^+(F(A))\) the full subcategory of \(K^+(F(A))\) whose objects are the complexes \(X'\) such that \(H^i(\mathrm{Gr}_j(X')) \in B\) for any pair of integers \((i, j)\): \(K_B^+(F(A))\) is a localizing triangulated subcategory of \(K(F(A))\) [cf. [7]. (I. §5)]. We we will designate by \(\mathrm{D}_B^+F(A)\) the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{D}\,F(A)\) whose objects are the lower bounded complexes \(X'\) such that \(H^i(\mathrm{Gr}_j(X')) \in B\) for any pair \((i, j)\): according to (loc. cit.), \(\mathrm{D}_B^+F(A)\) identifies with the category of fractions \(K_B^+(F(A))_\Sigma \cap K_B^+(F(A))\).

The “forgetting filtrations” functor: \[ \iota : K^+(F(A)) \longrightarrow K^+(A) \] is triangulated. Furthermore, it follows from \([[5]. I. (4.7)]\) that \(\iota(f)\) is a quasi-isomorphism if \(f \in \Sigma\) and \(\iota(K_B^+(F(A)) \subset K_B^+(A))\). The functor \(\iota\) extends thus into a triangulated functor \[ (2.5.2.1) \qquad \iota : \mathrm{D}^+F(A) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^+(A) \] such as \(\iota(\mathrm{D}_B^+F(A)) \subset \mathrm{D}_B^+(A)\).

Finally note that there exists a canonical spectral functor \(\mathrm{D}_B^+F(A) \to B\), resulting in \(H^n \circ \iota\), and whose term \(E_1\) is given by \(H^{p+q} \circ \mathrm{Gr}_p\) [cf. [5] I. (4.2)].
We now return to the notations of (2.3) assuming \(D = \Delta\): \(Z'\) will designate the complex of \(\mathrm{Add}(\Delta)\) defined by (2.3.9.1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.5.3)

Let \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be a ringed topos. The functor \(\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} : \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \to \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) has a canonical factorization \[ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \xrightarrow{\ {}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*}\ } \mathrm{D}^+F(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \xrightarrow{\ \iota\ } \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] Furthermore, for any integer \(i\), the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times D), \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{R}^+(e_i^*)} \ar[dr]_-{{}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*}} & & \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \\ & \mathrm{D}^+F(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[ur]_-{\mathrm{Gr}_i} & } \]

Consider the functor \(C^+(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) \xrightarrow{\ (\ )_s\ } C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\): if \(X''\) is an object of \(C^+(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\), we equip \((X'')_s\) with its second canonical filtration [cf. [5]. I.4.8]; we thus obtain a factorization: \[ (2.5.3.1) \qquad (\ )_s : C^+(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) \longrightarrow C^+(F(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) \xrightarrow{\ \iota\ } C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] which moves to categories \(K^+\): \[ (2.5.3.2) \qquad (\ )_s : K^+(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) \longrightarrow K^+(F(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) \xrightarrow{\ \iota\ } K^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] because a homotopy of \(C^+(C^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) induces a filtered homotopy.

We then have a diagram: \[ (2.5.3.3) \qquad \xymatrix@R=1.6em@C=2.4em{ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times \Delta), \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*}} \ar@{-->}[dr] & & \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \\ & \mathrm{D}^+F(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[ur]_-{\iota} & \\ & K^+(F(S, \mathcal{O}_S)) \ar[u] \ar[dr]^-{\iota} & \\ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times \Delta), \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[uuu] \ar[ur] \ar[rr]_-{(\ )_s \circ K^+(\varepsilon_{Z'*})} & & K^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[uuu] } \] and we check that there exists a functor \[ {}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*} : \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(S \times \Delta), \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^+F(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] and only one making it commutative (2.5.3.3).
The rest of the proposition is obvious.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.5.4)

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed semi-simplicial topos and \(\theta : (E, A) \longrightarrow (S \times \Delta, \mathcal{O}_S)\) an increase: for any integer \(i\), we denote by \(\theta_i : (E_i, A_i) \to (S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) the ringed topos morphism induced by \(\theta\) above by \(i\).

The functor \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_* : \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) has a factorization canonical \[ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \xrightarrow{\ {}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_*\ } \mathrm{D}^+F(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \xrightarrow{\ \iota\ } \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \] such that for any integer \(i\), the canonical diagram \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{R}^+ e_i^*} \ar[d]_{{}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_*} & & \mathrm{D}^+(E_i, A_i) \ar[d]^{\mathrm{R}^+\theta_{i*}} \\ \mathrm{D}^+F(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[rr]_-{\mathrm{Gr}_i} & & \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) } \] is essentially commutative.
Results from the above and from (1.3.12).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.5.5)

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed semi-simplicial topos and \(\theta : (E, A) \longrightarrow (S \times \Delta, \mathcal{O}_S)\) a cohomological 1-descent increase. Either \(H : (S, \mathcal{O}_S) \longrightarrow (R, \mathcal{O}_R)\) a morphism of ringed topos. Then there exists a functor spectral of \({}'\mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(R, \mathcal{O}_R)\): \[ E_1^{pq} = \mathrm{R}^q(H \circ \theta_p)_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+\theta_p^* \Longrightarrow \mathrm{R}^{p+q}H_* \] called spectral descent functor.

Let \(H \circ \theta : (E, A) \longrightarrow (R \times \Delta, \mathcal{O}_R)\) be the canonically deduced increase of \(\theta\) by composition with \(H\); we have \[ (2.5.5.1) \qquad \overline{(H \circ \theta)}_* = H_* \circ \overline{\theta}_* \] so that the diagram \[ (2.5.5.2) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=2.4em{ \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*)} \ar[dr]_-{\mathrm{R}^+H_*} & & \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \ar[dl]^-{\mathrm{R}^+\overline{(H \circ \theta)}_*} \\ & \mathrm{D}^+(R, \mathcal{O}_R) & } \] is essentially commutative.

\((\mathrm{R}^+\overline{(H \circ \theta)}_* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}^+H_* \circ \mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_*\) and \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S)} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*))\). Thanks to (2.5.4), we have for all \(i\), an essentially commutative diagram: \[ (2.5.5.3) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.4em@C=2.6em{ \mathrm{D}^+(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{L}^+\overline{\theta}^*} \ar[ddr]_-{\mathrm{R}^+H_*} & \mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(E), A) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{R}^+(e_i^*)} \ar[d]|-{{}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\overline{(H \circ \theta)}_*} & \mathrm{D}^+(E_i, A_i) \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{R}^+(H \circ \theta_i)_*} \\ & \mathrm{D}^+F(R, \mathcal{O}_R) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Gr}_i} \ar[d]_-{\iota} & \mathrm{D}^+(R, \mathcal{O}_R) \\ & \mathrm{D}^+(R, \mathcal{O}_R) & } \] The announced spectral functor is obtained by considering the spectral functor canonical on \(\mathrm{D}^+F(R, \mathcal{O}_R)\) which we compose with \({}^{F}\mathrm{R}^+\overline{(H \circ \theta)}_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{\theta}^*)\).
We leave it to the reader to verify the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.5.6)

With the previous notations, the term \(E_2^{pq}\) of the functor preceding spectral is written: \[ \check{H}^p \circ \mathrm{R}^q((H \circ \theta)_*) \circ \mathrm{L}^+\overline{\theta}^* \] where \(\check{H}^p : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(R \times \Delta), \mathcal{O}_R) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(R, \mathcal{O}_R)\) is the functor that associates with any functor \(\Delta \xrightarrow{\ F\ } \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(R, \mathcal{O}_R)\) the homology object in degree \(p\) of the associated complex (noted \(\varepsilon_{Z'*}(F)'\) in (2.3.3)).

We now return to the terminology introduced in (2.4): thanks to sorite (2.5.2), all the previous considerations will be transcribed word for word by placing the letter \(G\) as a subscript wherever it makes sense. The details are left to the reader; we obtain in particular:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (2.5.7)

Let \(\theta : X \longrightarrow C_S^D\) be a cohomological 1-descent increase relative to \(G\) and let \(h : S \longrightarrow R\) be a morphism of \(B\); there is a spectral functor of \(\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\) in \(G_R\): \[ E_1^{pq} = \mathrm{R}_G^q(h \circ \theta)_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\theta_p^* \Longrightarrow \mathrm{R}_G^{p+q}h_* \] with \(E_2^{pq} = \check{H}^p \circ \mathrm{R}_G^q\underline{\Gamma}((h \circ \theta)_*) \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{\theta}^*\).

footnotes.html: (∗) for any complex \(K'\), \(K'[-n]\) denotes the complex \(T^{-n}(K')\), where \(T\) is the translation functor.
(∗) For any abelian category \(A\), the letter \(Q\) designates the canonical functor \(K^+(A) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}^+(A)\).

blocks.html: 3. Descent criteria
3.0 Ratings
In all that follows, we will retain the notations of (2.4). We We will always assume that the finished fiber products are representable in \(B\).
We will further assume that \(B\) satisfies the following condition:
(3.0.0) Direct sums exist in \(B\), are disjoint, universal and families of effective descent and \(G^{o}\)-effective descent [cf. [4] (9.23) (9.25) and (9.27)].
(3.0.1) Let us now recall some classic notations on objects semi-simplicials of a category [cf. ([3] Chap. II) and (V. appendix)].
Let \(E\) be a category possessing finite projective limits \(\Delta^{o}E\) denotes the category of semi-simplicial objects of \(E\) (cf. (1.2.6)).

Let \(n\) be an integer: we denote by \(\Delta_n\) (resp. \(\Delta_n^+\), \(\Delta_n^-\)) the subcategory full of \(\Delta\) (resp. \(\Delta^+\), \(\Delta^-\)) formed by objects \([p]\) such as \(p \leq n\).

The restriction functor \[ (3.0.1.1) \qquad i_n^* : \Delta^{o}E \longrightarrow \Delta_n^{o}E \] has a right adjoint \(i_{n*}\), since finite projective limits exist in \(E\) (cf. (1.2.10)). We note \(\mathrm{cosk}_n\) and we call order coskeleton functor \(n\) functor \(i_{n*} \circ i_n^*\); by abuse of notations, we will also use the notation \(\mathrm{cosk}_n\) for the functor \(i_{n*}\).

For any integer \(p\), let us denote by \(\Delta_{n[p]}\) (resp. \(\Delta_{n[p]}^+\)) the subcategory full \(\Delta_{[p]}\) (resp. \(\Delta_{[p]}^+\)) objects of \(\Delta\) (resp. \(\Delta^+\)) above \([p]\), defined by objects \([q]\) above \([p]\) such as \(q \leq n\); we leave It is up to the reader to verify that we have: \[ (3.0.1.2) \qquad (\mathrm{cosk}_n(X))_p = \varprojlim_{\Delta_{n[p]}} X_q \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{\Delta_{n[p]}^+} X_q \]

(3.0.2) Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be two objects of \(\Delta^{o}E\) and \(f, g : X \rightrightarrows X'\) be two morphisms. A homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\) consists of the data for all \(n\) of an application \(h_n : \mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([n], [1]) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_E(X_n, X'_n)\) checking the following two conditions:
\[ (3.0.2.1) \qquad h_n(\partial^1) = f_n \quad \text{et} \quad h_n(\partial^{o}) = g_n \quad \text{pour tout } n. \]

(3.0.2.2) for any arrow \([n] \longrightarrow [p]\) in \(\Delta\), we have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=2.5em{ \mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([p], [1]) \ar[r] \ar[dd] & \mathrm{Hom}_E(X_p, X'_p) \ar[dr] & \\ & & \mathrm{Hom}_E(X_p, X'_p) \\ \mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([n], [1]) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_E(X_n, X'_n) \ar[ur] & } \]

N.B.: The relation thus introduced on the set of morphisms of \(X\) in \(X'\) is not an equivalence relation; we can overcome this drawback by introducing the notion of compound homotopy [cf. [3] ch. III]: we will not use not this last notion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.0.2.3)

Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be two objects of \(\Delta^{o}E\), \(f, g : X \rightrightarrows X'\) two morphisms and \(F : E \to C\) a functor where \(C\) is an abelian category. A simplicial homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\) induces a homotopy on the morphisms cochain complexes canonically associated with \(F(f)\) and \(F(g)\).
We are brought back to the case where \(C\) is the category of modules on a ring and we apply \([[5] I. (3.7.1)]\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.0.2.4)

Let \(n\) be an integer, \(X\) and \(X'\) two objects of \(\Delta_n^{o}E\). Let \(f\) and \(g\) two morphisms of \(X\) in \(X'\) such that \(f_p = g_p\) for \(p < n\): then there exists a simplicial homotopy from \(\mathrm{cosk}_n(f)\) to \(\mathrm{cosk}_n(g)\).

We define \(h_p : \mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([p], [1]) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(X_p, X'_p)\) by the application value constant \(f_p = g_p\) for \(p < n\), then \(h_n : \mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([n], [1]) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(X_n, X'_n)\) sending all elements of \(\mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([n], [1])\), except \(\partial^{o}\), on \(f_n\), and \(\partial^{o}\) on \(g_n\). We then notice that \(\mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([k], [1]) = \varprojlim_{\substack{[q] \to [k] \\ q \geq n}} \mathrm{Hom}([q], [1])\) for \(k > n\), which allows canonically defining \(h_k\).
3.1 Comparison of two increases from the point of view of cohomological 1-descent

Let \(S\) be an object of \(B\): we denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{plat}}(D^{o}, B/S)\) the subcategory full of \(\mathrm{Hom}(D^{o}, B/S)\) whose objects are flat augmentations [cf. (1.2.6) and (2.4.3.1)].

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.1.1)

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^D\) and \(X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } C_S^D\) be two objects of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{plat}}(D^{o}, B/S)\). Let \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) be a morphism above \(S\) (i.e. a morphism of \(\mathrm{Hom}(D^{o}, B/S)\)); it corresponds in a natural way to a morphism functional \[ \eta_G^f : \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}'_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{u}'^* \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{u}^* \] such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ & \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}'_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{u}'^* \ar[dd]^{\eta_G^f} \\ 1_{\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)} \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ & \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{u}^* } \]
Moreover, if \(D = \Delta\), \(\eta_G^f\) only depends on the homotopy class (cf. (3.0.2)) from \(f\) into \(\mathrm{Hom}(\Delta^{o}, B/S)\).
It is clear that we can assume that \(G = \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G})\) for the construction of \(\eta_G^f\).

Let \(I\) be the category defined by the diagram type \[ (3.1.1.1) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow 1 \] We designate by \(r_o : D \longrightarrow I \times D\) (resp. \(r_1\)) the fully faithful functor defined by \(r_o(i) = (0, i)\) (resp. \(r_1(i) = (1, i)\)).

Under canonical isomorphism \[ (3.1.1.2) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}((I \times D)^{o}, B/S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I^{o}, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(D^{o}, B/S)) \] the data of (3.1.1) define a flat increase \[ (3.1.1.3) \qquad [X\,|_f\,X'] \xrightarrow{\ ufu'\ } C_S^{I \times D} \]

Let \(F\) be an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_S)\), the canonical morphism \[ (3.1.1.4) \qquad e_{I \times D}^*(F) \longrightarrow \underline{\Gamma}((ufu')_*) \circ \underline{\Gamma}((ufu')^*)(e_{I \times D}^*(F)) \] can be interpreted as a commutative triangle in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{C_S^D}), \mathfrak{G})\): \[ (3.1.1.5) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ & \underline{\Gamma}(u'_*) \circ \underline{\Gamma}(u'^*)(e_D^*(F)) \ar[dd] \\ e_D^*(F) \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ & \underline{\Gamma}(u_*) \circ \underline{\Gamma}(u^*)(e_D^*(F)) } \] hence a functorial morphism \(\overline{u}'_* \circ \overline{u}'^* \xrightarrow{\ \alpha_f\ } \overline{u}_* \circ \overline{u}^*\) such that the diagram \[ (3.1.1.6) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ & \overline{u}'_* \circ \overline{u}'^* \ar[dd]^{\alpha_f} \\ 1_{\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_S)} \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ & \overline{u}_* \circ \overline{u}^* } \] be commutative.

We subsequently obtain a functorial morphism such as the diagram \[ (3.1.1.7) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ & K^+(\overline{u}'_*) \circ K^+(\overline{u}'^*) \ar[dd]^{K^+(\alpha_f)} \\ 1_{K^+(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_S)} \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ & K^+(\overline{u}_*) \circ K^+(\overline{u}^*) } \] be commutative.
(Note that \(K^+(\alpha_f)\) can also be obtained formally by addition using the isomorphism \(K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(f^*)) \circ K^+(\overline{u}'^*) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } K^+(\overline{u}^*)\)).

Let \(F'\) be a complex of \(K^+(S)\) and \(\xi : K^+(\overline{ufu'}^*)(F') \to J'\) be a quasi-isomorphism such that, for any integer \(n\), \(J^n\) is totally acyclic object by object (cf. (1.3.13)). According to (loc. cit.) the canonical arrows \[ K^+(\overline{u}'^*)(F') \longrightarrow r_1^*(J') \quad \text{et} \quad K^+(\overline{u}^*)(F') \longrightarrow r_o^*(J') \] are quasi-isomorphisms (see (1.2.8) for notations).
