We subsequently obtain a morphism \[ \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}'_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+\overline{u}'^*(F') \xrightarrow{\ \eta^f(F')\ } \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+\overline{u}^*(F') \] such that the following diagram is commutative in \(\mathrm{D}^+(S)\): \[ (3.1.1.8) \qquad \xymatrix@!0@C=11em@R=5.5em{ & Q \circ K^+(\overline{u}'_*)(r_o^*(J')) \ar[rr]^-{\sim} \ar[dd] & & \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}'_* \circ Q(r_o^*(J')) \ar[dd] \\ Q \circ K^+(\overline{u}'_*) \circ K^+(\overline{u}'^*)(F') \ar[ur] \ar[rr]^(0.56){\sim} \ar[dd]_{Q(K^+(\alpha_f)(F'))} & & \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}'_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+\overline{u}'^*(F') \ar[ur] \ar[dd]^(0.36){\eta^f(F')} & \\ & Q \circ K^+(\overline{u}_*)(r_1^*(J')) \ar[rr]^(0.62){\sim} & & \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_* \circ Q(r_1^*(J')) \\ Q \circ K^+(\overline{u}_*) \circ K^+(\overline{u}^*)(F') \ar[ur] \ar[rr] & & \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+\overline{u}^*(F') \ar[ur] & } \]
It is clear that \(\eta^f(F')\) does not depend on the chosen resolution and we checks that it is functorial in \(F'\) varying in \(\mathrm{D}^+(S)\).
To complete the proof of (3.1.1), it is enough to show the lemma next:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.1.1.9)

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) and \(X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } C_S^\Delta\) be two objects of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{plat}}(\Delta^{o}, B/S)\). Let \(f, g : X \longrightarrow X'\) be two morphisms in \(\mathrm{Hom}(\Delta^{o}, B/S)\): if there exists a simplicial homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\), we have \(\eta^f = \eta^g\).

Let \(h_n : \mathrm{Hom}_\Delta([n], [1]) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{B/S}(X_n, X'_n)\) be a simplicial homotopy of \(f\) to \(g\). Let \(\overline{I \times D}\) be the category obtained from \(I \times D\) by adding the arrows \((1, n) \to (0, n)\) corresponding bijectively to \(h_n\,(\mathrm{Hom}([n], [1]))\) and the relationships imposed by the compatibility of \(h_n\) for \(n\) variable. The data of lemma define a flat augmentation \([X\,|_f\,X'] \xrightarrow{\ t\ } C_S^{\overline{I \times D}}\). Let \(F'\) be a complex of \(C^+(S)\) and \(J'\) a resolution of \(C^+(\overline{e}^*)(F')\) such that for any integer \(n\), \(J^n\) is totally acyclic object by object: there exists a simplicial homotopy between \[ C^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u'_*))(r_o^*(J')) \rightrightarrows C^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u_*))(r_1^*(J')) \] hence a homotopy when we move on to “condensed” complexes. (see (3.0.2.3)).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (3.1.2)

With the notations of (3.1.1), we will say that \(f\) is a equivalence for \(G\)-1-cohomological descent if \(\eta_G^f\) is an isomorphism.

(3.1.3) We will note in the following by \(L_i : \Delta \to \Delta \times \Delta\) (resp. \(c_i : \Delta \to \Delta \times \Delta\)) the canonical functor defined by \([n] \to [n] \times [i]\) (resp. \([n] \to [i] \times [n]\)).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.1.4)

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^{\Delta \times \Delta}\) and \(X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } C_S^{\Delta \times \Delta}\) be two objects from \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{plat}}((\Delta \times \Delta)^{o}, B/S)\). Let \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) be a morphism above \(S\): we assumes that \(f * \mathrm{id}_{L_i} : X \circ L_i \to X' \circ L_i\) (resp. \(f * \mathrm{id}_{c_i} : X \circ c_i \to X' \circ c_i\)) is a equivalence for the \(G\)-1-cohomological descent for any integer \(i\). So \(f\) is an equivalence for \(G\)-1-cohomological descent.

According to the description of \(\eta^f\) (see demonstration of (3.1.1)) it is to show that a certain morphism of triple complexes induces an isomorphism on the cohomology of condensed complexes: a standard reasoning by sequence spectral data then allows us to conclude.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.1.5)

Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be two objects of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{plat}}(\Delta^{o}, B/S)\). Either \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) a functorial morphism such that \(f_n : X_n \longrightarrow X'_n\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent. Then the canonical morphism \([[X\,|_f\,X']] \longrightarrow [[X'\,|_{\mathrm{id}}\,X']]\) (cf. (1.2.7)) is an equivalence for the \(G\)-1-cohomological descent.
Results from (3.1.4) and the following lemma, the proof of which is left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.1.5.1)

Let \(f : R \longrightarrow S\) be a flat morphism, \(Y \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) and \(Y \xrightarrow{\ v\ } C_R^\Delta\) two flat augmentations such that the following diagram is commutative in \(\mathrm{Hom}(\Delta^{o}, B)\): \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=2em{ Y \ar[rr]^-{v} \ar[dr]_-{u} & & C_R^\Delta \ar[dl]^-{f} \\ & C_S^\Delta & } \] If \(v\) is an increase of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent, it is an equivalence for the \(G\)-1-cohomological descent.
In applications we will combine (3.1.4) and (3.1.5) with the result next:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.1.6)

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^{\Delta \times \Delta}\) be a flat increase. So that \(u\) is an increase of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent, it is enough that \(X \circ L_i \xrightarrow{\ u * \mathrm{id}_{L_i}\ } C_S^\Delta\) (resp. \(X \circ c_i \xrightarrow{\ u * \mathrm{id}_{c_i}\ } C_S^\Delta\)) is for any integer \(i\).
This results from the explicit construction of \(\mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}_*\) (cf. (2.3.10)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (3.1.7)

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) be an increase so that \(u\) is \(G\)-1-cohomological descent, it is necessary and sufficient that \([[X\,|_{\mathrm{id}}\,X]] \longrightarrow C_S^{\Delta \times \Delta}\) be.
3.2 Location criteria

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.2.1)

Let \(Y \xrightarrow{\ v\ } C_S^\Delta\) be an increase of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent. So that a flat rise \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) is \(G\)-1-down cohomological, it suffices that it becomes so after all the base changes \(Y_n \longrightarrow S\).

By means of base changes \(Y_n \longrightarrow S\), we construct an object semi-simplicial double \(X \times_S Y\) increased to \(S\). According to (3.1.4) and (3.1.5.1) the canonical morphism \(X \times_S Y \longrightarrow [[X\,|_{\mathrm{id}}\,X]]\) is an equivalence for the \(G\)-1-descent cohomological: by virtue of (3.1.7), it suffices to show that the increase of \(X \times_S Y\) to \(S\) is of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent. But this results from the fact that the canonical morphism \(X \times_S Y \longrightarrow [[Y\,|_{\mathrm{id}}\,Y]]\) is an equivalence for the \(G\)-1-descent cohomological: we still use (3.1.4), (3.1.5.1) and (3.1.7).

(3.2.2) Either \[ (3.2.2.1) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ Y' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & Y \ar[d]^{f} \\ X' \ar[r]_-{g} & X } \] a commutative diagram in \(B\) and let \(F\) be an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_S)\): it exists a morphism and only one \(\varphi(F) : g_*(f^*(F)) \longrightarrow f'^*(g'_*(F))\) such that the diagram following (in \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G})\)), or commutative: \[ (3.2.2.2) \qquad \xymatrix@R=3em@C=1.6em{ f^*(F) \ar[rr] \ar[d] & & F \ar[d] \\ g_*(f^*(F)) \ar[dr] & & g'_*(F) \ar[dl] \\ & f'^*(g'_*(F)) & } \]

If we now assume that all the arrows in (3.2.2.1) are flat, we define in the same way a morphism, called a change of base: \[ (3.2.2.3) \qquad \underline{g}_G : \mathrm{L}_G^+ g_* \circ \mathrm{R}_G^+ f^* \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}_G^+ f'^* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+ g'_* \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (3.2.2.4)

We say that the diagram (3.2.2.1) verifies the theorem of base change relative to \(G\) if \(f\), \(g\), \(f'\), \(g'\) are flat morphisms and if \(\underline{g}_G\) is an isomorphism.

(3.2.3) Let \(h : S \longrightarrow S'\) be a flat morphism in \(B\), \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) and \(X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } C_{S'}^\Delta\) two flat increases. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) be a morphism functorial such as the diagram (in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\Delta^{o}, B)\)) \[ (3.2.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d]_{u} & X' \ar[d]^{u'} \\ C_S^\Delta \ar[r]_-{h_c} & C_{S'}^\Delta } \] is commutative. (\(h_c\) denotes the constant functorial morphism defined by \(h\)).

Let \(K'\) be a complex of \(\mathrm{D}^+(S')\): the morphism \[ \eta_G^f(K') : \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}'_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+\overline{u}'^*(K') \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{h_c \circ u})_* \circ \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{h_c \circ u})^*(K') \] induced, taking into account the identities: \[ \mathrm{R}^+(\overline{h_c \circ u})_* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}^+h_* \circ \mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_* \quad \text{et} \quad \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{h_c \circ u})^* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{L}^+(\overline{u}^*) \circ \mathrm{L}^+h^* \] a morphism \(\mathrm{ch}_G^{f,h}(K')\), functorial into \(K'\), such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ (3.2.3.2) \qquad \xymatrix@R=3.5em@C=1.2em{ \mathrm{L}_G^+h_* \circ \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}'_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{u}'^*(K') \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{ch}_G^{f,h}(K')} & & \mathrm{R}_G^+\overline{u}_* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+\overline{u}^* \circ \mathrm{L}_G^+h_*(K') \\ & \mathrm{L}_G^+h_*(K') \ar[ul]^-{\mathrm{L}_G^+h_*(\alpha(K'))} \ar[ur]_-{\alpha(\mathrm{L}_G^+h_*(K'))} & } \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.2.3.3)

Suppose that, for any integer \(i\), the diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X_i \ar[r]^-{f_i} \ar[d]_{u_i} & X'_i \ar[d]^{u'_i} \\ S \ar[r]_-{h} & S' } \] verifies the base change theorem with respect to \(G\). Then \(\mathrm{ch}_G^{f,h}\) is an isomorphism.

We can calculate \(\mathrm{ch}_G^{f,h}(K')\) in the following way: we consider the increase \([X\,|_f\,X'] \xrightarrow{\ (h_c \circ u)_f\, u'\ } C_{S'}^\Delta\) (cf. (3.1.1.2)) and we choose a resolution \(J'\) of \(K^+(\overline{(h_c \circ u)fu'}^*)(K')\) such that, for any integer \(n\), \(J^n\) is completely acyclic object by object.

We obtain a quasi-isomorphism \[ \theta : K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(f^*))(r_o^*(J')) \longrightarrow r_1^*(J') \] and a morphism \[ t : K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(h_c^*)) \circ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u'_*))(r_o^*(J')) \longrightarrow K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u_*))(r_1^*(J')) \] which admits factorization: \[ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(h_c^*)) \circ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u'_*))(r_o^*(J')) \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u_*)) \circ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(f^*))(r_o^*(J')) \xrightarrow{\ K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u_*))(\theta)\ } K^+(\underline{\Gamma}(u_*))(r_1^*(J')) \] and \(\mathrm{ch}_G^{f,h}(K')\) is obtained by taking the image of \(t\) by the functor \(\mathrm{R}^+\varepsilon_{Z'*}\).

However, the previous factorization shows that, \(t\) is identified "object by object" to base change morphisms relating to diagrams: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X_i \ar[r]^-{f_i} \ar[d]_{u_i} & X'_i \ar[d]^{u'_i} \\ S \ar[r]_-{h} & S' } \] we deduce, by spectral sequences, that \(\mathrm{ch}_G^{f,h}(K')\) is an isomorphism, which completes the demonstration.
This leads us to a second location criterion:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.2.4)

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) be an object of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{plat}}(\Delta^{o}, B/S)\). So that \(u\) is an increase of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent, it suffices that it becomes after "sufficient for \(G\)"(∗) of flat basis changes \(h : S' \longrightarrow S\) such as Cartesian diagrams \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X'_i \ar[r] \ar[d] & X_i \ar[d] \\ S' \ar[r]_-{h} & S } \] verify the change of base theorem with respect to \(G\).
The proposition (3.2.4) is obvious from (3.2.3.3), given of the diagram (3.2.3.2).
3.3 Properties of cohomological descent morphisms
In this issue, we assume that the set of flat morphisms in \(B\) is stable under change of base.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.3.1)

\(i = 1, 2\).

  1. a)

    Any flat morphism that has a section is a morphism of \(G\)-2-universal cohomological descent.

  2. b)

    Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a flat morphism, \(g : S' \longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of \(G\)-\(i\)-cohomological descent, \(X' = X \times_S S'\), \(f' = f_{(S')} : X' \longrightarrow S'\). So that \(f\) or of \(G\)-\(i\)-universal cohomological descent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) the either.

  3. c)

    If the compound of two morphisms \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\), \(g : Y \longrightarrow Z\) is \(G\)-\(i\)-universal cohomological descent, \(g\) is \(G\)-\(i\)-cohomological descent universal.

  4. d)

    The compound of two morphisms of \(G\)-\(i\)-universal cohomological descent is a morphism of \(G\)-\(i\)-universal cohomological descent.

  5. e)

    If \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) and \(g : Y \longrightarrow Y'\) are two \(S\)-morphisms of \(G\)-\(i\)-descent universal cohomological, \(f \times_S g\) is \(G\)-\(i\)-universal cohomological descent.

  6. f)

    Let \((u_\alpha : X_\alpha \longrightarrow Y_\alpha)_{\alpha \in A}\) be a family of morphisms of \(G\)-\(i\)-descent cohomological. Then \(\coprod u_\alpha : \coprod_{\alpha \in A} X_\alpha \longrightarrow \coprod_{\alpha \in A} Y_\alpha\) is a morphism of \(G\)-\(i\)-cohomological descent.
Demonstration. Concerning the effective descent relative to \(G\) we refer to [4].

a) By means of a section \(s\) of \(f\) we compare the semi-simplicial objects increased to \(S\): \([X\,|_f\,S]\) and \([S\,|_{\mathrm{id}}\,S]\); thanks to (3.0.2.4) we obtain a equivalence for \(G\)-1-cohomological descent.
b) Result of (3.2.1).
c) Result of a) and b).

d) Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) and \(g : Y \longrightarrow S\) be two morphisms of \(G\)-1-descent universal cohomology. Let us set \(h = g \circ f\) and consider the fiber product: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ Y \ar[d]_{g} & R \ar[l]_-{h'} \ar[d]^{g'} \\ S & X \ar[l]^-{h} } \] \(g'\) has a section \(s : X \longrightarrow R\) such as \(h' \circ s = f\). According to c) \(h'\) is of \(G\)-1-universal descent and it is the same for \(h\) according to b).
e) Formally results from b) and d).

f) Let \((Z_\lambda)_{\lambda \in \Lambda}\) be a family of objects of \(B\). It results from the hypotheses (3.0.0) that we have a canonical equivalence \[ \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}\Big(\mathscr{E}_{\coprod_\lambda Z_\lambda}^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_{\coprod_\lambda Z_\lambda}\Big) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \prod_\lambda \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_{Z_\lambda}^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_{Z_\lambda}) \] inducing an equivalence \[ G_{\coprod_\lambda Z_\lambda} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \prod_\lambda G_{Z_\lambda} \] Furthermore, if \((Q_\lambda)_\lambda \in \prod_\lambda \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_{Z_\lambda}^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_{Z_\lambda})\) is totally acyclic, \(Q_\lambda\) is totally acyclic for all \(\lambda\).

Let now be \((u_\lambda : X_\lambda \longrightarrow Y_\lambda)_{\lambda \in \Lambda}\) a family of morphisms of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent: since the direct sums in \(B\) are disjoint and universal, we have for everything \(n\), an identification \[ \Big[\coprod_\lambda X_\lambda \,\Big|_{\coprod_\lambda u_\lambda}\, \coprod_\lambda Y_\lambda\Big]_n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \coprod_\lambda [X_\lambda \,|_{u_\lambda}\, Y_\lambda]_n \] which is functorial in \(n\). We then deduce from the preceding remarks and explicit calculation of \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{\theta}_*\) (cf. (2.3)) that \(\coprod_\lambda u_\lambda\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-descent cohomological: details are left to the reader. We also leave the latter the care of checking that \(\coprod_\lambda u_\lambda\) remains of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent after all change of base if it is the same of \(u_\lambda\) for all \(\lambda\).

(3.3.2) If we associate with each object \(X\) of \(B\) all the families of morphisms \((X_\alpha \longrightarrow X)_{\alpha \in A}\) such that \(\coprod_\alpha X_\alpha \longrightarrow X\) is a morphism of \(G\)-\(i\)-universal cohomological descent we define, by virtue of (3.3.1), a pretopology on \(B\).

The topology generated by this pretopology is called the topology of the \(G\)-\(i\)-cohomological descent. It follows from \([(3.3.1)\text{-}c)]\) that the morphisms coverings for this topology are exactly the morphisms of \(G\)-\(i\)-descent universal cohomology. Finally, note that the topology of the \(G\)-2-descent cohomological is less fine than the topology of the \(G^{o}\)-descent [cf. [4] (6.23)].

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem (3.3.3)

We assume that all the arrows of \(B\) are flat. Or \(S\) an object of \(B\): any hyperoverlap of \(S\), for the topology of the \(G\)-1-descent cohomological, of which all objects are representable (see V appendix) defines an increase in \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent.
The demonstration is done in two steps: let us specify that all coskeletons will be calculated in the category \(B/S\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.3.3.1)

Let \(X\) be an object of \(\mathrm{Hom}(\Delta^{o}, B/S)\) so that \(X\) is of 1-cohomological descent (resp. universal), it is enough that \(\mathrm{cosk}_n(X)\) is for all \(n\) big enough.

Let in fact be \(\alpha_n : X \longrightarrow \mathrm{cosk}_n(X)\) the canonical morphism: we verify whereas if \(K'\) is a complex of \(\mathrm{D}_{G_S}^+(S)\) such that \(H^j(K') = 0\) for \(j < N\), \(H^i(\eta_G^{\alpha_n})\) is an isomorphism for \(i < N+n\), hence the assertion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.3.3.2)

Let \(n\) be an integer \(\geq 0\), \(X\) and \(X'\) two simplicial objects from \(B/S\). Let \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) be a morphism. We assume that:

  1. (i)

    \(X \longrightarrow \mathrm{cosk}_{n+1}(X)\) is an isomorphism.

  2. (ii)

    \(X' \longrightarrow \mathrm{cosk}_{n+1}(X')\) is an isomorphism.

  3. (iii)

    \(f_i : X'_i \longrightarrow X_i\) is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n\).

  4. (iv)

    \(f_{n+1}\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent.

Then if \(X\) is \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent, it is likewise of \(X'\).
It is clear that (3.3.3.1) and (3.3.3.2) prove theorem (3.3.3) by recurrence.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.3.3.3)

Under the assumptions of (3.3.3.2), the morphisms \(f_p\) are all of \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent

This is trivial for \(p \leq n+1\). For \(p > n+1\), \(X_p\) (resp. \(X'_p\)) can be written as \(\varprojlim_{\Delta^+_{n+1[p]}} X_q\) (resp. \(\varprojlim_{\Delta^+_{n+1[p]}} X'_q\)). Gold for every arrow \(\iota : i \longrightarrow j\) of \(\Delta^+_{n+1[p]}\) we have a commutative diagram with exact lines: \[ \xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=2.8em{ K'_\iota \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\alpha'_\iota} & \displaystyle\prod_{r \in \mathrm{ob}(\Delta^+_{n+1[p]})} X'_2 \ar[r] & X'_j \ar[r] & X'_i \ar[d]^{f_i} \\ K_\iota \ar[r] & \displaystyle\prod_{\mathrm{ob}(\Delta^+_{n+1[p]})} X_2 \ar[r] & X_j \ar[r] & X_i } \] whose left square is Cartesian (either \(i = n+1 = j\) and \(\iota = \mathrm{id}\), or else \(i < n+1\) and \(f_i\) is an isomorphism). Then using (3.3.1) we see that \(\alpha_\iota\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent. We complete the demonstration noting that \(f_p\) identifies with the canonical morphism \(\bigcap_\iota K'_\iota \xrightarrow{\ \times \alpha_\iota\ } \bigcap_\iota K_\iota\).

Let \([X'/X]^p\) be the iterated fiber product \((p+1)\)-fold of \(X'\) above \(X\). Thanks to (3.3.3.3), it is enough to check (3.3.3.2) after a change of base \([X'/X]^p \longrightarrow X\). After such a change of basis, the hypotheses of (3.3.3.2) are still verified, and moreover \(f_{n+1}\) admits a section. We can then apply (3.0.2.4) to complete the demonstration.
Regarding the effective descent, we have:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (3.3.4)

Let \(X : \Delta^{o} \longrightarrow B/S\) be a functor. We assume that \(X_o \longrightarrow S\) is a morphism of \(G^{o}\)-2-universal descent as well as the morphisms \(X_{n+1} \longrightarrow (\mathrm{cosk}_n(X))_{n+1}\) for \(n = 0, 1\). Then the functor \(X\) is \(G^{o}\)-2-faithful and the remainder after any base change (i.e. \(X\) defines an increase of universal effective descent within the meaning of (2.4.11)).
From [4] (7.12), it suffices to see that \(i_2^*(X)\) is \(G^{o}\)-2-faithful and To do this, we use the following lemmas:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.3.4.1)

Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be two functors \(\Delta_2^{o} \longrightarrow B/S\) and a diagram commutative: \[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.8em{ X'_2 \ar@<0.8ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-0.8ex>[r] \ar[d]_{k} & X'_1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar@{=}[d] & X'_o \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & S \ar@{=}[d] \\ X_2 \ar@<0.8ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-0.8ex>[r] & X_1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & X_o \ar[r] & S } \] such that \(k\) is a morphism of \(G^{o}\)-0-descent. Then if \(X\) is \(G^{o}\)-2-faithful, the same is true of \(X'\).
Obvious.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (3.3.4.2)

Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be two functors \(\Delta_2^{o} \longrightarrow B/S\) and a diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix@C=3.2em@R=2.8em{ X'_2 \ar@<0.8ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-0.8ex>[r] \ar[d]_{f_2} & X'_1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r]^{\partial^{o}} \ar@<-0.5ex>[r]_{\partial^1} \ar[d]^{f_1} & X'_o \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & S \ar@{=}[d] \\ X_2 \ar@<0.8ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-0.8ex>[r] & X_1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & X_o \ar[r] & S } \] such that \(f_1\) is of \(G^{o}\)-1-descent and \(f_2\) is of \(G^{o}\)-0-descent. We assume to more than \(X'_2 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathrm{cosk})_1(X'))_2\). Then if \(X\) is \(G^{o}\)-2-faithful, it is even from \(X'\).
Let \(X''_1 = X'_1 \times_{X_1} X'_1\), \(\partial_o : X''_1 \longrightarrow X'_1\) and \(\partial_1 : X''_1 \longrightarrow X'_1\) both projections.

Thanks to the definition of a coskeleton, we define an arrow \(\varphi : X''_1 \longrightarrow X'_2\) such that: \[ \begin{cases} \partial_o \circ \varphi = \underline{\partial}_o \\ \partial_1 \circ \varphi = \underline{\partial}_1 \\ \partial_2 \circ \varphi = \sigma_o \partial_1 \underline{\partial}_o = \sigma_o \partial_1 \underline{\partial}_1 \end{cases} \]

Let now be a descent data on \(X'\); hence an object on \(X_o\), two objects on \(X_1\), an isomorphism between them on \(X'_1\). Thanks to \(\varphi\), we see that the two reciprocal images of these isomorphisms on \(X''_1\) are equal. Since \(f\) is 1-descent, we catch an isomorphism between the two objects on \(X_1\); this isomorphism is a descent data (thanks to the fact that \(X'_2 \longrightarrow X_2\) is of 0-descent), and we won.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (3.3.5)

We assume that all the arrows of \(B\) are flat: all hypercovering of \(S\), for the topology of the \(G\)-2-cohomological descent, whose objects are representable, defines an increase of \(G\)-2-descent universal cohomology.

footnotes.html: (∗) The expression "sufficient for \(G\)" means that there exists a family \((S_\alpha \xrightarrow{\ h_\alpha\ } S)_{\alpha \in A}\) of flat morphisms satisfying the previous conditions and such that the family of functors \((h_{\alpha*} : G_S \to G_{S_\alpha})_{\alpha \in A}\) is conservative.

blocks.html: 4. Examples
4.1 Sheaves of abelian groups on topological spaces

(4.1.0) In this issue \(\mathrm{Top}\) designates the category of topological spaces [elements of the fixed universe \(\mathcal{U}\)]: we define a category \(\mathscr{E}\) bifibered in duals of topos above \(\mathrm{Top}\) taking as objects the pairs \((X, F)\), where \(X\) is a topological space and \(F\) a sheaf of sets on \(X\), and for morphisms the couples \((f, \varphi) : (X, F) \longrightarrow (Y, H)\) where \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is an application continues and \(\varphi : H \longrightarrow f_*(F)\) a sheaf morphism. We take for \(\mathfrak{G}\) the section of \(\mathscr{E}^{o}\) above \(\mathrm{Top}^{o}\) which associates with each topological space the constant beam \(\mathbf{Z}_X\): we will pose \(G = \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G})\) so that, for any space topological \(X\), \(G_X\) identifies with the category of sheaves of abelian groups on \(X\).

Recall that a morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is separated if the diagonal of \(X \times_Y X\) is closed. A proper morphism is a separate and universally closed (note that this definition is more restrictive than that of Bourbaki).
The proof of the "change of base theorem" below is inspired by \(([5] II (4.11.1))\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem (4.1.1)

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism, \(F\) be a sheaf abelian on \(X\) and a Cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ Y' \ar[r]_-{g} & Y } \] So, the canonical application (XII 4.2) or (3.2.2.3) \[ g^* \mathrm{R}^i f_* F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{R}^i f'_* g'^* F \] is an isomorphism.
This theorem is equivalent to the following corollary (the corollary is obtained by doing \(Y' = (\text{Point})\); the theorem is obtained by applying the corollary to \(f\) and \(f'\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (4.1.2)

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism, \(F\) be a sheaf abelian on \(X\) and \(y \in Y\). The canonical application \[ (\mathrm{R}^i f_* F)_y \longrightarrow H^i(f^{-1}(y), F) \] is bijective.

By definition, for \(U\) traversing the neighborhoods of \(Y\), we have \[ (\mathrm{R}^i f_* F)_y = \varinjlim_U H^i(f^{-1}(U), F) . \] Since \(f\) is closed, the \(f^{-1}(U)\) form a fundamental system of neighborhoods of \(f^{-1}(y)\) and (4.1.3) results from the following lemma.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (4.1.3)

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(K \subset X\) and \(F\) be a sheaf abelian on \(X\). We assume that \(K\) is compact and that two distinct points any of \(K\) have, in \(X\), disjoint neighborhoods. So, for \(U\) browsing the neighborhoods of \(K\), we have \[ (4.1.3.1) \qquad \varinjlim_U H^i(U, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^i(K, F) \]
We will first deal with the case \(i = 0\). In this case, it is clear that (4.1.3.1) is injective. For surjectivity, we will use:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (4.1.4)

Under the hypotheses of (4.1.3), if \(A\) and \(B\) are two closed of \(K\) and \(W\) a neighborhood of \(A \cap B\) in \(X\), there exist neighborhoods \(U\) and \(V\) of \(A\) and \(B\) in \(X\) such that \(U \cap V \subset W\).

We will first deal with the case where \(A\) is reduced to a point \(a\). The assertion is trivial if \(a \in B\) (take \(U = W\)). If \(a \notin B\) it exists for each \(b \in B\) of the disjoint open neighborhoods \(U_b\) and \(V_b\) of \(a\) and \(b\) in \(X\). We takes for \(V\) a finite union of \(V_b\) which covers \(B\), and for \(U\) the intersection of the corresponding \(U_b\).

In the general case, for each \(a \in A\), there exist open neighborhoods \(U_a\) and \(V_a\) from \(a\) and \(B\) in \(X\) with \(U_a \cap V_a \subset W\). We take for \(U\) a finished meeting of \(U_a\), which covers \(A\), and for \(V\) the corresponding intersection of \(V_a\).

Let's return to (4.1.3). If \(s \in H^0(K, F)\), there is an open overlay \(U_i\) of \(K\) in \(X\) and \(s_i \in H^0(U_i, F)\) such as \(s_i = s\) on \(U_i \cap K\). We can assume the \(U_i\) in finite number. Let \((K_i)\) be a closed covering of \(K\), with \(K_i \subset K \cap U_i\). Let \(W_y\) be the open of \(U_i \cap U_j\) where \(s_i = s_j\); we have \(K_i \cap K_j \subset W_{ij}\). Let us apply (4.1.4) to all pairs \((K_i, K_j)\) and to \(W_{ij}\): we finds open \(V_{ij}\) with \(K_i \subset V_{ij} \subset U_i\) and \(V_{ij} \cap V_{ji} \subset W_{ij}\). Either \(U'_i = \bigcap_j V_{ij}\): we have \(K_i \subset U'_i \subset U_i\), and \(s_i = s_j\) on \(U'_i \cap U'_j\). The \(s_i\) are therefore stick to the neighborhood of \(K\) meeting of \(U'_i\), and (4.1.3.1) is surjective (therefore bijective) for \(i = 0\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (4.1.5)

If \(F\) is flabby, the beam \(F|_K\) on \(K\) is soft.
Let \(A \subset K\) be a closed one in \(K\). Any section of \(F\) on \(A\) extends to a neighborhood, according to the above. Since \(F\) is flaccid, it extends to \(X\), and a fortiori to \(K\).

Let us prove (4.1.3). Let \(F^*\) be a flaccid resolution of \(F\). We have \[ \varinjlim_U H^i(U, F) = \varinjlim_U H^i(\Gamma(U, F^*)) = H^i \varinjlim_U \Gamma(U, F^*) = H^i \Gamma(K, F^*) = H^i(K, F) . \] (the equality \(H^i \varinjlim_U \Gamma(U, F^*) = H^i \Gamma(K, F^*)\) results from (4.1.3) for \(i = 0\), cf. (4.1.5)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (4.1.6)

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a proper and surjective morphism of spaces topological. Then, \(f\) is \(G\)-1-cohomological descent.
Results from (4.1.2) and (3.2.4).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (4.1.7)

Let \(\mathfrak{u} = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a locally finite closed covering of a topological space \(X\). Then the canonical morphism \(f : \coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-cohomological descent.
Indeed \(f\) is proper and surjective. By applying (2.5.6), we find the spectral sequence of \([[5] II (5.2.4)]\) (see the introduction).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (4.1.8)

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a local surjective space homeomorphism topological. Then \(f\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent.
By localization on \(Y\) (cf. (3.2.4)), we are brought back to the case where \(f\) has a section.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (4.1.9)

Let \(\mathfrak{u} = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) be an open covering of a space topological \(X\). Then the canonical morphism \(f : \coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) is a morphism of \(G\)-1-universal cohomological descent.
The spectral descent sequence then gives \([[5] II (5.4.1)]\) (see the Introduction).
The demonstrations of (VIII. 9) show that we can replace "1-cohomological descent" by "2-cohomological descent" in all statements which precede.
4.2 Quasi-consistent modules on diagrams

(4.2.0) Let \((\mathrm{Sch})_S\) be the category whose objects are the element schemas of \(\mathcal{U}\) and the arrows the quasi-compact and quasi-separate morphisms: we define a category \(\mathscr{E}\) bifibered into duals of topos above \((\mathrm{Sch})_S\) taking for objects the pairs \((X, F)\) where \(X\) is a diagram and \(F\) a beam on \(X\) (for the Zariski topology), and for morphisms the couples \((f, \varphi) : (X, F) \longrightarrow (Y, G)\), where \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is a continuous map and \(\varphi : G \longrightarrow f_*(F)\) is a morphism of bundles. We take for \(\mathfrak{G}\) the section of \(\mathscr{E}^{o}\) above \((\mathrm{Sch})^{o}\) which associates to every diagram its structural beam; we take for \(G\) the subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G})\) such that for any schema \(X\), \(G_X\) is the category of modules quasi-consistent on \(X\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (4.2.1)

Any morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) faithfully flat quasi-compact and quasi-separate is a morphism of \(G\)-2-universal cohomological descent.

According to \([\mathrm{S.G.A\ I\ (VIII\ 5.2)}]\), \(f\) is a morphism of \(G^{o}\)-descent effective. By virtue of \([\mathrm{E.G.A\ IV\ (1.7.21)}]\), we can apply (3.2.4) to the square Cartesian: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X \times_Y X \ar[r] \ar[d] & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ X \ar[r]_-{f} & Y } \]
4.3 Spread beams on the diagrams

(4.3.0) Let \((\mathrm{Sch})\) be the category of all schemas belonging to \(\mathcal{U}\): we therefore defines a category \(\mathscr{E}\) bifibered into duals of topos above \((\mathrm{Sch})\) in taking as objects the couples \((X, F)\), where \(X\) is a diagram and \(F\) a sheaf spreads over \(X\), and for morphisms the pairs \((f, \varphi) : (X, F) \longrightarrow (Y, G)\), where \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is a schema morphism and \(\varphi : G \longrightarrow f_*(F)\) is a morphism of beams.

Let \(n\) be an integer \(\geq 0\): we denote by \(\mathfrak{G}_n\) the section of \(\mathscr{E}^{o}\) above \((\mathrm{Sch})^{o}\) which associates with any scheme \(X\) the constant beam \((\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})_X\). We will pose \(F_n = \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_n)\) so that, for any schema \(X\), \(F_{n_X}\) identifies with the category bundles of \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\).

(4.3.1) Let now \((\mathrm{Sch})_S\) be the subcategory of \((\mathrm{Sch})\) whose objects are the element diagrams of \(\mathcal{U}\) and whose arrows are the quasi-compact morphisms and almost separated. We denote by \(\mathscr{E}_S\) the bifibered category in duals of topos above \((\mathrm{Sch})_S\) inferred from \(\mathscr{E}\) by base change \((\mathrm{Sch})_S \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Sch})\); we also designate by \(\mathfrak{G}_o\) the restriction of \(\mathfrak{G}_o\) to \((\mathrm{Sch})_S\). Or \(G \subset \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_o)\) the fibered and cofibered subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}_S^{o}, \mathfrak{G}_o)\) such that for any schema \(X\), \(G_X\) or the category of abelian torsion sheaves on \(X\): the reader will note that the condition (2.4.1.1) is verified.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (4.3.2)

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a surjective proper morphism. Then \(f\) is a \(F_n\)-2-universal cohomological descent morphism for \(n \geq 1\). Considered as a morphism of \((\mathrm{Sch})_S\), \(f\) is a morphism of \(G\)-2-cohomological descent universal.

According to (VIII. (9.4)) \(f\) is a morphism of \(F^{o}\)-effective descent and according to (XII. (5.1)), we can apply (3.2.4) to the Cartesian diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X \times_Y X \ar[r] \ar[d] & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ X \ar[r]_-{f} & Y } \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (4.3.3)

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a surjective morphism of schemas. We suppose that one of the following hypotheses is verified:

  1. a)

    \(f\) is integer.

  2. b)

    \(Y\) is locally Noetherian, \(f\) is universally open and locally of finished presentation.

  3. c)

    \(f\) is flat and locally finite in presentation.

Then \(f\) is a morphism of \(F_n\)-2-cohomological descent, for all \(n \geq 0\).
a) We copy the proof of (4.3.2) by replacing (XII. (5.1)) by (VIII. (5.6)).

b) The question being local on \(Y\), we can assume that \(Y\) is Noetherian. We apply (E.G.A. IV. (14.5.10)). By (3.3.1) and a) we return to the case where all point \(y\) of \(Y\) has a neighborhood \(U\) such that \(f^{-1}(U) \longrightarrow U\) has a section and we win by (3.2.4).
c) This involves showing that \(f\) is a morphism of \(F\)-1-cohomological descent (we know from (VIII. (9.4)) that \(f\) is a morphism of \(F^{o}\)-effective descent).

By localization on \(Y\) (cf. (3.2.4)), we can assume that \(Y\) is affine ring \(A\). We can then write \(\operatorname{Spec} A = \varprojlim_{i \in I} \operatorname{Spec} A_i\), where \(I\) is a filtering ordered set and \(A_i\) a Noetherian ring for all \(i\), with \(X = \varprojlim X_i\), where, for all \(i\), \(X_i\) is a flat, locally presentation schema finite on \(\operatorname{Spec} A_i\) such that \(f_i : X_i \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} A_i\) is surjective; moreover, the diagrams: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X \ar[r] \ar[d]_{f} & X_i \ar[d]^{f_i} \\ Y \ar[r]_-{u_i} & \operatorname{Spec} A_i } \] are Cartesian.

Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be an abelian sheaf on \(Y\): we have \(\mathfrak{F} = \varinjlim_i u_i^* u_{i*}(\mathfrak{F})\) so that, by passing to the standard limit, we are brought back to the case where \(\mathfrak{F}\) comes from of a beam on \(\operatorname{Spec} A_i\) for a certain \(i\), and b) allows us to conclude.

If \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is integer, we have \(\mathrm{R}^q f_* = 0\) for \(q > 0\). We easily concludes that it is not necessary to limit oneself to bounded complexes lower to descend using \(f\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (4.3.5)

Let \((X_\alpha \xrightarrow{\ u_\alpha\ } X)_{\alpha \in A}\) be a covering family for the slack topology. Then \(\coprod_{\alpha \in A} X_\alpha \longrightarrow X\) is a morphism of \(F_n\)-2-descent universal cohomology for all \(n \geq 0\).
Indeed, there is a factorization \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=1em{ & \coprod_{\alpha \in A} X_\alpha \ar[dr] & \\ X \ar[rr]_-{h} \ar[ur] & & X } \] where \(h\) is a surjective slack morphism.
5. Applications
5.1 Construction of simplicial objects

(5.1.0) Up to and including number (5.1.3), \(H\) is a category where the limits non-empty finite projectives exist and where finite sums exist, are disjoint and universal. If \(X : \Delta^{o} \longrightarrow H\) is a simplicial object of \(H\), we will note \(d_i^k : X_k \longrightarrow X_{k-1}\) (resp. \(s_i^k : X_k \longrightarrow X_{k+1}\)) for \(i \in [k]\) and \(0 \leq k\) the arrows corresponding to the "faces" operators \(\partial_k^i : [k-1] \longrightarrow [k]\) (resp. degeneracy operators \(\sigma_k^i : [k] \longrightarrow [k+1]\)) (cf. [3] II 2).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (5.1.1)

A simplicial object \(X : \Delta^{o} \longrightarrow H\) of \(H\) is \(\sigma\)-split if it exists a family of sub-objects \(NX_j\) of \(X_j\) (\(j \geq 0\)) such that morphisms \[ h_i : \coprod_{\substack{\mathrm{Hom}_{\Delta^-}([i], [j]) \\ j \leq i}} NX_j \longrightarrow X_i \] are isomorphisms.

Similarly, a simplicial object \(k\)-truncated \(X : \Delta_k^{o} \longrightarrow H\) is \(\sigma\)-split if there exists \(NX_j\) (\(0 \leq j \leq k\)) checking the previous condition for \(i \leq k\).

The \(NX_j\) are only determined by \(X\). Indeed, if \(X\) is \(\sigma\)-split, the degeneracy operators \(s_\iota^{k-1} : X_{k-1} \longrightarrow X_k\) are isomorphisms of \(X_{k-1}\) with direct factors of \(X_k\), and \[ NX_k = \bigcap_\iota (\text{complément de } s_\iota^k(X_{k-1})) . \] For \(k = 0\), \(NX_o = X_o\).
(5.1.2) For \(X\) a simplicial object \((n+1)\)-truncated \(\sigma\)-split from \(H\), we we will denote by \(\alpha_n(X)\) the triple consisting of

1. a)

    the restriction \(i_n^*(X)\) from \(X\) to \(\Delta_n^{o}\).

  2. b)

    \(NX_{n+1}\),

  3. c)

    the obvious application of \(NX_{n+1}\) in \((\mathrm{cosq}_n(X))_{n+1}\)
This triple \((Y, N, \beta)\) checks the following condition:
(*) \(Y\) is a simplicial object \(n\)-truncated \(\sigma\)-split from \(H\), and \(\beta\) is a application of \(N\) in \((\mathrm{cosq}\, Y)_{n+1}\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.1.3)

Let \((Y, N, \beta)\) be a triple checking (*) above.

  1. (i)

    Up to unique isomorphism, there exists one and only one \(X : \Delta_{n+1}^{o} \longrightarrow H\) \(\sigma\)-split, with \(\alpha_n(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (Y, N, \beta)\),

  2. (ii)

    It is the same to give a morphism \(f\) of \(X\) in an object truncated simplicial \(Z : \Delta_{n+1}^{o} \longrightarrow H\), or to give

    1. a)

      a morphism \(f' : Y \longrightarrow i_n^*(Z)\);

    2. b)

      a morphism \(f'' : N \longrightarrow Z_{n+1}\) such as the diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ N \ar[r] \ar[d]_{f''} & (\mathrm{cosq}_n Y)_{n+1} \ar[d]^{f'} \\ Z_{n+1} \ar[r] & (\mathrm{cosq}_n Z)_{n+1} } \] is commutative.
Let's build \(X\).

We set: \(Y'_n = (\mathrm{cosk}_n Y)_{n+1}\) and we note \(d'^{n+1}_i : Y'_{n+1} \longrightarrow Y_n\) (resp. \(s'^n_i : Y_n \longrightarrow Y'_{n+1}\)) face operators (resp. degeneracy) obtained by functoriality.

+ We set \(Y_{n+1} = N \amalg \Big(\coprod_{\substack{\mathrm{Hom}_{\Delta^-}([n+1], [\iota]) \\ \iota \leq n}} NY_\iota\Big)\) and let \(\alpha : Y_{n+1} \longrightarrow Y'_{n+1}\) be the arrow whose components are \(\beta\) and the arrows \(NY_\iota \longrightarrow Y'_{n+1}\) for any epimorphism \([n+1] \longrightarrow [\iota]\) with \(\iota \leq n\).

+ Let \(s_i^n : Y_n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \coprod_{\substack{\mathrm{Hom}_{\Delta^-}([n], [\iota']) \\ \iota' \leq n}} NY_{\iota'} \longrightarrow X_{n+1}\) the arrow induced by \(\sigma_n^i\), so that \[ \text{(I)} \qquad \alpha \circ s_i^n = s'^n_i . \] We pose \[ \text{(II)} \qquad d_i^{n+1} = d'^{n+1}_i \circ \alpha . \]

+ To show that we thus define an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\Delta_{n+1}^{o}, H)\), it is enough, according to \([[3]. II(2.4)]\) to check the formulas \[ \text{a)} \qquad d_i^n \cdot d_j^{n+1} = d_{j-1}^n \cdot d_i^{n+1} \qquad i < j \] \[ \text{b)} \qquad s_i^n \cdot s_j^{n-1} = s_{j+1}^n \cdot s_i^{n-1} \qquad i \leq j \] \[ \text{c)} \qquad d_i^{n+1} \cdot s_j^n = \begin{cases} s_{j-1}^{n-1} \cdot d_i^n & i < j \\ \mathrm{id} & i = j \text{ ou } i = j+1 \\ s_j^{n-1} \cdot d_{i-1}^n & i > j+1 \end{cases} \]
Formulas a) and c) are obvious by virtue of (I) and (II), because we know that a) and c) are verified if we replace \(d_i^{n+1}\) by \(d'^{n+1}_i\) and \(s_i^n\) by \(s'^n_i\).
Formula b) is verified by construction.
We leave it to the reader to verify that the object \(X\) obtained verifies 5.1.3.

+ We take care that the functor constructed in the demonstration of 5.1.3, from the category of triples verifying (5.1.2) (*) in simplicial objects \(n{+}1\)-truncated, is faithful but not fully faithful.

Proposition 5.1.3 provides a very convenient method for constructing by induction of simplicial objects of \(H\). At the end of this issue, we let's formalize this remark in the form that will be used in 5.2 and 5.3.
(5.1.4) Let \(E \xrightarrow{\ \pi\ } B\) be a functor such that for any object \(b\) of \(B\), the fiber category \(E_b\) is non-empty and satisfies the conditions of (5.1.0).
Let \(Q\) be a property of an object of \(E\), stable by isomorphism: on assumes that the following conditions are met:

1. a)

    For every object \(b\) of \(B\) there exists an object \(x\) of \(E_b\) satisfying \(Q\).

  2. b)

    For any object \(b\) of \(B\) and any pair \((x, y)\) of objects of \(E_b\) checking \(Q\), there exists an object \(z\) of \(E_b\) checking \(Q\) and morphisms \(z \longrightarrow x\), \(z \longrightarrow y\) in \(E_b\).

  3. c)

    For any morphism \(b' \xrightarrow{\ t\ } b\) of \(B\) and any object \(x\) of \(E_b\) checking \(Q\), there exists an object \(x'\) of \(E_{b'}\), checking \(Q\) and a morphism \(x' \longrightarrow x\) above \(t\).
Let on the other hand be \(P\) a property of an arrow of \(E\) above a stable identity by isomorphism: we assume that the following conditions are carried out:

1. d)

    For any object \(b\) of \(B\) and any object \(x\) of \(E_b\) there is an arrow \(y \longrightarrow x\) in \(E_b\) checking \(P\).

  2. e)

    For any object \(G\) of \(B\), any diagram in \(E_b\) of the form \[ \xymatrix@R=1.6em@C=2.5em{ u \ar[rr]^-{f} & & x \\ & z \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ v \ar[rr]_-{g} & & y } \] where \(f\) and \(g\) satisfy \(P\), can be completed in a commutative diagram in \(E_b\) \[ \xymatrix@R=1.6em@C=2.5em{ u \ar[rr]^-{f} & & x \\ w \ar[r]^-{h} \ar[u] \ar[d] & z \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ v \ar[rr]_-{g} & & y } \] where \(h\) checks for \(P\).

  3. f)

    For any morphism \(b' \xrightarrow{\ t\ } b\) of \(B\) and any morphism \(x \xrightarrow{\ f\ } y\) of \(E_b\) satisfying \(P\), there exists a morphism \(x' \xrightarrow{\ f'\ } y'\) of \(E_{b'}\), satisfying \(P\) and a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ x' \ar[r]^-{\alpha} \ar[d]_{f'} & x \ar[d]^{f} \\ y' \ar[r]_-{\beta} & y } \] where \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are morphisms above \(t\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (5.1.5)

With the notations of (5.1.4), we will say that an object \(X\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\Delta^{o}, E)\) checks the condition (APQ) if the following conditions are filled:

  1. (i)

    The image of \(\pi \circ X\) is the constant simplicial object defined by an object \(G\) from \(B\).

  2. (ii)

    As an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\Delta^{o}, E_b)\), \(X\) is \(\sigma\)-split.

  3. (iii)

    \(X_o\) checks the condition \(Q\).

  4. (iv)

    For any integer \(n\), the arrow \(NX_{n+1} \longrightarrow (\mathrm{cosq}_n X)_{n+1}\) checks the condition \(P\).

(5.1.6) We designate by \(E_{(APQ)}\) the subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\Delta^{o}, E)\) whose objects are the simplicial objects verifying (APQ) and whose morphisms are the morphisms \(X \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } Y\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\Delta^{o}, E)\) such that \(\pi(\alpha_n) : \pi(X_n) \longrightarrow \pi(Y_n)\) is the same morphism of \(B\) for all \(n\). There then exists a canonical functor \[ \overline{\pi} : E_{(APQ)} \longrightarrow B . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.1.7)

The functor \(\overline{\pi}\) is surjective. Additionally, for any object \(b\) of \(B\) and any pair \((X, Y)\) of objects of \(E_{(APQ),b}\), there exists a diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=1.5em{ & Z \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ X & & Y } \] in \(E_{(APQ),b}\).
The proof is trivial from (5.1.3), under the assumptions do in (5.1.4).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.1.8)

Let \(C\) be a category and \(F\) be a functor \(E_{(APQ)} \longrightarrow C\); we introduce the following conditions for \(F\):

  1. (i)

    For any object \(b\) of \(B\), the restriction of \(F\) to \(E_{(APQ),b}\) factors through the coarse category(∗) defined by the objects of \(E_{(APQ),b}\).

  2. (ii)

    For any morphism \(t : b' \longrightarrow b\) of \(B\) and any pair \((X' \longrightarrow X, Y' \longrightarrow Y)\) of morphisms over \(t\), the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ F(X') \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\alpha'} & F(X) \ar[d]^{\alpha} \\ F(Y') \ar[r] & F(Y) } \] where \(\alpha\) and \(\alpha'\) are the arrows determined by condition (i).

Then, the functor \(G \longmapsto G \circ \overline{\pi}\) induces an equivalence between the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(B, C)\) and the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_{(APQ)}, C)\) defined by the functors verifying conditions (i) and (ii).
The details of the demonstration are left to the reader. Let's indicate simply the way we proceed to show that the previous functor is essentially surjective.

Let \(F : E_{(APQ)} \longrightarrow C\) be a functor satisfying conditions (i) and (ii) for any object \(b\) of \(B\), we choose an object \(X\) of \(E_{(APQ),b}\) and we put \(\overline{F}(b) = F(X)\); we then check that \(\overline{F}(b)\) varies functionally with \(b\).
5.2 Singular cohomology of a finite type scheme on a field of zero characteristic
In this number, \(k\) will designate a body with zero characteristics.

(5.2.0) We will designate by \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/k\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Ann}\)) the category of schemas locally of finite type on \(k\) (resp. complex analytical spaces). All simplicial objects of \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/k\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Ann}\)) will be considered as objects simplicials of \(\mathrm{Top}\) thanks to the canonical functor \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/k \to \mathrm{Top}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Ann} \to \mathrm{Top}\)). We keep the data from (4.1.0) relating to cohomological descent.
Following the usual notations, we will denote \(S \longrightarrow S^{\mathrm{an}}\) the functor canonical \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/\mathbf{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ann}\) defined in G.A.G.A.

(5.2.1) Let \(X\) be a simplicial object of \(\mathrm{Sch}/k\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Ann}\)): the compatibilities usual for the construction of the De Rham complex of a schema morphism (resp. analytical spaces) make it possible to construct a complex \(\Omega^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{X/k}\) (resp. \(\Omega_{X/\mathbf{C}}\)) of \(\mathrm{D}^+(\underline{\Gamma}(\overline{X}), \mathbf{Z})\) such that for any integer \(i\), \(\mathrm{R}^+e_i^*(\Omega^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{X/k})\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^+e_i^*(\Omega_{X_i/\mathbf{C}})\)) or the De Rham complex \(\Omega^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{X_i/k}\) (resp. \(\Omega_{X_i/\mathbf{C}}\)).

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) be a simplicial object of \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/k\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Ann}\)) with of an increase. We define \(H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(u)\) (resp. \(H_{\mathrm{DR}}(u)\) and \(H(u, \mathbf{C})\)) as being the hypercohomology groups of the complex \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_*(\Omega^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{X/k})\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_*(\Omega_{X/\mathbf{C}})\) and \(\mathrm{R}^+\overline{u}_*(\mathbf{C})\)).

(5.2.2) If \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\) is a semi-simple object of \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/\mathbf{C}\), we have of three canonical morphisms: \[ H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(u) \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } H_{\mathrm{DR}}(u^{\mathrm{an}}) \] \[ H(u^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C}) \xrightarrow{\ \beta\ } H_{\mathrm{DR}}(u^{\mathrm{an}}) \] \[ H(S^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C}) \xrightarrow{\ \gamma\ } H(u^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C}) \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.2.3)

Suppose, with the notations of (5.2.2) that \(X_n\) is smooth for all \(n\): then \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are isomorphisms. If more \(X^{\mathrm{an}} \xrightarrow{\ u^{\mathrm{an}}\ } C_{S^{\mathrm{an}}}^\Delta\) is a hypercoverage of \(S^{\mathrm{an}}\) for the topology of the 1-universal cohomological descent, \(\gamma\) is an isomorphism.

The fact that \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism follows from \([[6]. \text{Th. } 1']\). It results from Poincaré's lemma that \(\beta\) is an isomorphism. The last part of the proposal results from (3.3.3).

(5.2.4) We will now apply (5.1.4) in case \(B = \mathrm{Sch}_f/k\) and \(E\) is the category above \(B\) such that for any schema \(S\), the category fiber \(E_S\) is the category of diagrams of \(\mathrm{Sch}_f/k\) above \(S\), the arrows above an arrow of \(B\) being obvious. We will say that a morphism \(h : X \longrightarrow Y\) above \(S\) checks the property \(P\) if \(X\) is smooth on \(k\) and if \(h\) is composed of a finite number of morphisms satisfying one of the conditions following:

1. (i)

    be the sum of a family of splayed and surjective morphisms

  2. (ii)

    be the sum of a family of proper and surjective morphisms.
We will say that a schema \(X\) of \(E_S\) verifies the property \(Q\) if the structural morphism \(X \longrightarrow S\) checks the property \(P\).
We leave it to the reader to verify, via the resolution of the singularities, that all the conditions of (5.1.4) are verified.
The group data \(H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(u)\) defines a contravariant functor of the category \(E_{(APQ)}\) in the category of graded abelian groups, noted \(H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.2.5)

The functor \(H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}\) satisfies the conditions of (5.1.8).

By Lefschetz's principle, we are brought back to the case where \(k = \mathbf{C}\). In this case, for any morphism \(S' \xrightarrow{\ t\ } S\) and any pair \((u' \longrightarrow u, v' \longrightarrow v)\) of morphisms above \(t\), we have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=3em@C=3.5em{ H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(u') \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\gamma^{-1}\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\alpha} & H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(u) \ar[d]^{\gamma^{-1}\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\alpha} \\ H(S'^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C}) \ar[r]^-{H(t^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C})} & H(S^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C}) \\ H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(v') \ar[r] \ar[u]^{\gamma^{-1}\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\alpha} & H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}(v) \ar[u]_{\gamma^{-1}\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\alpha} } \] which completes the demonstration.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition (5.2.6)

The contravariant functor defined by (5.1.8) from \(H^{\mathrm{Zar}}_{\mathrm{DR}}\) will be noted \(H(*, k)\). For any schema \(S\) locally of finite type on \(k\), the group graded abelian \(H(S, k)\) will be called the singular cohomology of \(S\).

(5.2.7) We will end this issue with an illustration of the principles generals that were introduced there. Let us first recall that if \(S\) is a space analytically complex, the De Rham cohomology of \(S\), denoted \(H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}}(S)\) is by definition of the hypercohomology of the De Rham complex \(\Omega^{\bullet}_{S/\mathbf{C}}\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.2.8) (Blum-Herrera)

Let \(S\) be a locally finite type schema on \(\mathbf{C}\), the arrow canonical \[ H(S^{\mathrm{an}}, \mathbf{C}) \longrightarrow H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}}(S^{\mathrm{an}}) \] has a functorial retraction (in the category of graded abelian groups) in \(S\).

By virtue of (5.1.8) it suffices to consider the morphism \(H(*, \mathbf{C}) \circ \overline{\pi} \longrightarrow H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}} \circ \overline{\pi}\) obtained from the previous one by composition with \(\overline{\pi}\).

By using, for any increase \(X \xrightarrow{\ u\ } C_S^\Delta\), the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{L}^+\overline{u}^*(\Omega^{\bullet}_{S/\mathbf{C}}) \longrightarrow \Omega^{\bullet}_{X/\mathbf{C}}\), we define a functorial morphism \(H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}} \circ \overline{\pi} \longrightarrow H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}}\), so that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=2.5em{ H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}} & & H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{DR}} \circ \overline{\pi} \ar[ll] \\ & H(*, \mathbf{C}) \circ \overline{\pi} \ar[ul]^{\beta \circ \gamma} \ar[ur] & } \] which provides the desired retraction.
The statement (5.2.8) remains valid for any analytical space, as soon as we have the resolution of singularities in the analytical framework.
5.3 Mixed Hodge theories
We give here a result of a technical nature which plays a role key in [2].
\(k\) is a field with zero characteristic.

(5.3.1) Let \(\mathrm{Sch}_{fs}/k\) be the category of separate and finite-type schemes on \(k\); we denote by \(E \xrightarrow{\ \pi\ } \mathrm{Sch}_{fs}/k\) the category above \(\mathrm{Sch}_{fs}/k\) defined in the following way:

+ If \(S\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{fs}/k\), an object of \(E_S\) is a triple \((\overline{X}, X, i)\) where \(\overline{X}\) is a projective reduced schema on top of \(k\), \(X\) a proper schema above \(S\) and \(i : X \longrightarrow \overline{X}\) an open immersion above \(k\) such that \(i(X)\) is dense in \(\overline{X}\).

+ If \(t : S' \longrightarrow S\) is a schema morphism, \((\overline{X}, X, i)\) an object above of \(S\) and \((\overline{X}', X', i')\) an object above \(S'\), a morphism of \((\overline{X}, X, i)\) in \((\overline{X}', X', i')\) above \(t\) is a pair \((\overline{h}, h)\) where \(\overline{h} : \overline{X}' \longrightarrow \overline{X}\) is a morphism above \(k\), and \(h : X' \longrightarrow X\) a morphism above \(S\), such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ X' \ar[r]^-{i'} \ar[d]_{h} & \overline{X}' \ar[d]^{\overline{h}} \\ X \ar[r]_-{i} & \overline{X} } \]
N.B.: we note that it results from the cleanliness hypotheses that \(\overline{h}^{-1}(i(X)) = i(X')\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma (5.3.2)

For any object \(S\) of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{fs}/k\), the category \(E_S\) is non-empty, has non-empty projective limits and finite direct sums which are disjoint and universal.
The fact that the category \(E_S\) is non-empty results from Chow's lemma.
We will only indicate the construction of the direct product of two objects \((\overline{X}, X, i)\) and \((\overline{X}', X', i')\) from \(E_S\).

The canonical arrow \(j : X \times_S X' \longrightarrow \overline{X} \times_k \overline{X}'\) is an immersion and we denotes by \(T\) the reduced sub-schema of \(\overline{X} \times_k \overline{X}'\) having the underlying space image stickiness of \(j\): \((T, (X \times_S X')_{\mathrm{red}}, j_{\mathrm{red}})\) checks the property desired universality.

(5.3.3) Let \(S\) be an object of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{fs}/k\): we will say that an object \((\overline{X}, X, i)\) of \(E_S\) checks the property \(Q\) if \(\overline{X}\) is smooth on \(k\) and if \(i(X)\) is the complement of a normal crossing divider. We will say that a morphism \((\overline{h}, h) : (\overline{X}', X', i') \longrightarrow (\overline{X}, X, i)\) checks the property \(P\) if \(\overline{h}\) is surjective and if \((\overline{X}', X', i')\) checks the property \(Q\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal (5.3.4)

With the notations of (5.3.3), all the conditions of (5.1.4) are verified.
Conditions a) b) d) and c) are easily verified using the resolution of singularities. Moreover c) is a consequence of f): it therefore remains to check f).

It is a matter of seeing that any commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=3em{ Z \ar[r]^-{i_Z} \ar[d]_{h} & \overline{Z} \ar[d]^{\overline{h}} \\ X \ar[r]^-{i_X} \ar[d] & \overline{X} \ar[d] \\ S \ar[r] & k \\ S' \ar[u]^{t} & } \] where \((\overline{X}, X, i_X)\) and \((\overline{Z}, Z, i_Z)\) are objects of \(E_S\) with \(\overline{h}\) surjective, can complete in a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@!0@C=6em@R=4.5em{ & Z' \ar[rr] \ar[dd]|(0.35){h'} \ar[dl]_{i_{Z'}} & & Z \ar[dd]|(0.35){h} \ar[dl]^{i_Z} \\ \overline{Z}' \ar[rr] \ar[dd]_{\overline{h}'} & & \overline{Z} \ar[dd]|(0.65){\overline{h}} & \\ & X' \ar[rr] \ar[dl]_{i_{X'}} & & X \ar[dl]^{i_X} \\ \overline{X}' \ar[rr] & & \overline{X} & } \] (all above \(S' \xrightarrow{\ t\ } S\) and \(k\)) where \((\overline{X}', X', i_{X'})\) and \((\overline{Z}', Z', i_{Z'})\) are objects of \(E_{S'}\), with \(\overline{h}'\) surjective. We consider a diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2.4em@C=2.6em{ T \ar[r]^-{\varphi} \ar[d]_{i_T} & S' \ar[r]^-{t} & S \ar[d] \\ \overline{T} \ar[rr]_-{\psi} & & k } \] where \(\varphi\) is proper and \(\psi\) projective: by base changes we deduce one diagram: \[ \xymatrix@!0@C=8em@R=4.5em{ & Z \times_S T_{\mathrm{red}} \ar[rr] \ar[dd] \ar[dl]_{p} & & Z \ar[dd] \ar[dl] \\ \overline{Z} \times_k \overline{T}_{\mathrm{red}} \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & \overline{Z} \ar[dd] & \\ & X \times_S T_{\mathrm{red}} \ar[rr] \ar[dl]_{q} & & X \ar[dl] \\ \overline{X} \times_k \overline{T}_{\mathrm{red}} \ar[rr] & & \overline{X} & } \] (all above \(T \longrightarrow S' \longrightarrow S\) and \(\overline{T} \longrightarrow k\)) where \(p\) and \(q\) are immersions; it is then enough to replace \(\overline{Z} \times_k \overline{T}_{\mathrm{red}}\) and \(\overline{X} \times_k \overline{T}_{\mathrm{red}}\) to the closed images of \(p\) and \(q\) to have the desired diagram, which completes the demonstration of (5.3.4).

footnotes.html: (∗) A category is called coarse if for any pair \((x, y)\) of objects, \(\mathrm{Hom}(x, y)\) is reduced to one element and only one.
